fields.title: a11y with Marcy Sutton
fields.summary: Marcy is a Senior Front-End Engineer at Deque Systems, where she works on the axe-core team focusing on accessibility test integrations into web developer tooling.
fields.transcript:
Kent C. Dodds: [00:00] Hey, everybody. My name is Kent C. Dodds. I'm here with one of my really good friends, Marcy Sutton. We're going to be talking about accessibility and automated testing.

[00:12] Marcy, I want to give you an opportunity to introduce yourself a little bit. Help us get to know you. Why don't you introduce yourself for us?

Marcy Sutton: [00:21] Thanks so much for having me. I'm Marcy Sutton. I work at Deque Systems, on accessibility tools. I do a lot of public speaking, getting developers and designers on board with web accessibility, which is all about making the Web more accessible to people with disabilities.

[00:38] That is what I'm really passionate about. I love where JavaScript and HTML and CSS all come together. I tend to nerd out a lot on CSS Object Model and how that interfaces with JavaScript and automated testing. The technical side of accessibility is what I'm really into, but it's really in that end goal of making it more accessible to actual people with disabilities. You've always got to keep that end user in mind and not geek out too hard.

[01:09] Otherwise, you miss the real reason for all of it, is we're building stuff for people.

Kent: [01:15] I love that. I think that's a good life lesson, too. It's like don't forget your why. I think that you've got a pretty clear why. You've been working on accessibility-related stuff for a really long time. Can you tell us a little bit about why you care so much about accessibility, why does it matter to you so much?

Marcy: [01:37] I think deep, like if I really look inward and get honest about it, I think it's a selfish reason. If I develop some disability or I have a child with a disability, I would be paying it forward, but I'm doing it for the people who have disabilities from birth. I don't know. It's a really fulfilling thing to do my job in service of people who need better access.

[02:07] Increasingly, as services go online, like paying your bills or managing your insurance, all the things that we take for granted in life being easy to do online, if those services are not accessible, then someone with a disability can't do it independently.

[02:23] They have to get help. They have to share their ATM PIN number with a stranger and then potentially get defrauded. All of those details that we just go, "Eh, it's not accessible. Whatever." The reality of that is that someone might not be able to independently manage their own affairs. I'm always keeping that in the back of my mind.

[02:50] I work on accessibility from a higher level so that it trickles down to all of the developers out there making all of these products. I'm hoping that we can start to chip away at accessibility in all of our various projects, and really move the needle for people with disabilities. That's what gets me up in the morning.

Kent: [03:12] That's so cool. Your "why?" around accessibility is all rooted around empathy for other people. If anybody were to stop and just imagine their life if they were unable to see for a second...I think that's a good exercise, for us to imagine for yourself, "What if I couldn't...If I had no left hand?" Whatever it is, to develop a little bit of empathy for people.

[03:46] I think that's awesome what you're doing. I've known you for a long time and I loved watching you get even closer and closer to a position where you could really make a huge impact on accessibility with the things that you're working on. I want to talk about some of those things, too.

[04:03] One thing I wanted to get clear ahead of time was a lot of time people see the letters a11y. Could you explain that a little bit? What is a11y have to do with accessibility?

Marcy: [04:18] It's a numeronym. There are 11 letters between the A and the Y in accessibility. I don't know about you, but I like short things...on the Internet, typing a hashtag. Sometimes it's kind of a difficult word to spell correctly on the first try. A lot of members of the community have rallied around that a11y hashtag as a form of communication, a rallying cry. I have heard criticisms of it.

[04:49] Maybe it's not the most accessible way to sell accessibility. Even on things like Twitter, going on the a11y hashtag, you can see what people around the world are talking about with regards to accessibility. I'm a fan of it. I do try to spell it out. My own Twitter bio, I say accessibility because if you don't already know what a11y is, then I'm missing the opportunity to advocate for it.

[05:21] I go back and forth, but it's just short for accessibility, similar to "internationalization," i18n, it's a numeronym.

Kent: [05:31] A numeronym?

Marcy: [05:33] Yeah, word of the day.

Kent: [05:37] I want to ask a little bit about your work at Deque. What is Deque? I'm interested how Deque gets funding and stuff like that. Can you tell us a little bit about your company?

Marcy: [05:54] Deque, I think it started off as a computer science term, double ended queue, but it's morphed into the company's motto is digital equality. We're really aiming for digital equality. We only do Web accessibility and native mobile accessibility.

[06:16] We create tools for developers to get testing for accessibility, both manual and automated testing. There's other lines of business. That's the product side of Deque, but there's a whole group of accessibility subject-matter experts that are really the core.

[06:38] Where Deque started was doing an audit for a bank, an airline, or a smaller company so that they can either be proactive about accessibility, so that you're not worrying about...Really, the worry would be a user with a disability not able to use your site.

[06:59] The risk of a lawsuit, that's what gets business people talking sometimes. The real reason is that you want that person with a disability to be able to check out in your online store or be able to do their online banking without having to share their private information.

[07:16] There's those two sides. We get funding from doing accessibility testing work and some remediation, as well, which is a fancy word for fixing stuff.

[07:27] I'm in between now. I've recently moved from the product team to the developer services team. I do consulting, a lot of public speaking, but then I also have half of my body attached to the product team. I work on our JavaScript tools. It's a lot like a juggling act trying to do all of that, but I see that as the benefit of having lots of things I could work on.

Kent: [07:55] It sounds like you have a holistic view of what could benefit the company and, at the end of the day, the world, from an accessibility perspective. You just do whatever you feel will benefit everybody most. That's awesome.

[08:18] Some of these tools that you're building, like you mentioned for automated and manual testing, can you talk a little bit about the automated testing and what tools we have available to us?

Marcy: [08:33] The core of all of that Deque is the axe-core library. It's a JavaScript library. It's been around for five years or so. It's their latest iteration on a process of testing that Deque's been developing over time.

[08:51] It's not the first version of that, which I think is why it's been so successful. They learned a lot early on, and then made subsequent products that are revisions of that way of automated testing.

[09:08] Axe-core itself is a JavaScript file that you can insert into a browser. You can run an audit and it will find accessibility issues that can be detected through automation. There's limits to that, and I think this is partly what you wanted to talk about.

[09:24] You can test for things like are your imagines missing all attributes? Did you make up some attribute that you thought was a valid ARIA role and it's not actually valid? Before, when I got into accessibility, we didn't have tools like that, at least not freely available tools. There might have been enterprise things when I first got started 10 years ago.

[09:53] Now, it's so much easier to just open a browser extension. There is an axe browser extension. You can test in the way that you need to, whether it's just a quick, manual test in the browser, or you could inject it into your automated test suite so that it runs the full gamut, depending where the person doing the testing is sitting. What are your needs?

[10:20] Ultimately, I think the goal, at least of the product team, was trying to get developers to really bake this into their workflow, it's not like, "Oh, I forgot it this sprint." It's baked into your automated test. If somebody breaks a component for accessibility, your continuous integration server will flash red, and you'll know that you broke something.

[10:45] A way to solidify it in your process is to make it part of your automated testing, but that can only go so far. There's limits on what we can automate based on what's available in Web APIs. Does it require a human to review it?

[11:02] An example would be error validation. If you've got a really fancy client-side form, and the user has a wrong in there and you have a little error message, you can do really fancy things with binding that text message with ARIA to a form field so you associate them together.

[11:22] We could automate that potentially, but the quality of those messages, does it make sense to a human, what they need to fix? That piece of making that error message the clearest it can be, that's a human realm. That's not really a computer realm.

[11:40] Depending on what the challenge is and how it impacts the users, we can only get so far with automation. We can catch the low-hanging fruit that nobody should be messing up. We can definitely automate that.

Kent: [11:55] Let's talk about some of those things, just some examples and why they're a problem. I think a lot of people may not know the first thing about accessibility. They're just throwing a couple inputs on with maybe placeholders, maybe just text next to it.

[12:13] Can you give us a couple of examples of some low-hanging fruit -- whether or not you can actually automate testing that low-hanging fruit is another subject -- that we can do to make sure that our stuff is accessible?

Marcy: [12:28] I'd say the most common one that I see that would be both easy to fix and can be detected through automation is form labels, which you just mentioned. If you've got a form and you've got some inputs, if each of those inputs doesn't have a label, if we go back to my banking example, someone who's blind, if those form fields are not labeled, they don't know which one is which.

[12:51] They have to guess, they have to get help, or they just give up and don't use your site. If you properly associate a label, and there's multiple ways to do that -- a label element and some text wrapping the input is the easiest way -- that will expose, "Is it the first name field? Is it the credit card field? What field is that? Is it a checkbox that you need to uncheck to not get spam email?"

[13:18] That's the most common one even in popular...I won't name names, but there are some online learn-to-code sites and services that miss that still. It's everywhere, so I'd say form labels is number one.

Kent: [13:35] I'll shout out really quick. In the process of building my testing libraries, I built dom testing library and react testing library. One of the things that I really wanted to nail was testing things the way that a user would test it.

[13:52] When a user wants to fill out their username, they're going to look for, even if they have no disabilities that impact their interaction with the site, they're not going to look for the first input on the page. They're going to look for the input that's labeled user name.

[14:06] These libraries, you say, "Go get me the input that's labeled username," you'd getByLabel text username, and that will get you the field. This has made a really great impact for me in my work because, as I start adding tests to different applications that I'm working o here at PayPal, I notice, "Oh, that looks like a div, not a label."

[14:34] If you try to select an input by its label, you'll get an error that says, "Hey, I found a label, but it's not associated to an input," or something like that. There are a lot of things that we can do with our testing tools to make it the natural things.

[14:51] It ends up, for these testing tools, it makes it easier to test as well. It's easier to say, "Get me the field that's got this label," rather than, "Get me the first input," or whatever. I think our tools can really help us in testing this or automating some of these low-hanging fruit things. I think you had a couple others that you wanted to talk about, too. Sorry to interrupt.

Marcy: [15:17] No. There's definitely more to be said around using your automated testing to be a tool to uncover some accessibility problems.

[15:27] Color contrast I have to mention. There was a run of axe-core, which I mentioned earlier. That's the accessibility layer doing a lot of the testing in both Lighthouse and webhint.io, which was previously called Sonarwhal. I think it was Sonar. Then it was Sonarwhal. They've renamed it now as Webhint.

[15:52] The accessibility testing in those is axe-core, the tool that we developed at Deque. When that rule set from axe-core was run on the HTTP archive, the number one, far and away accessibility issue was color contrast.

Kent: [16:07] That's so interesting.

Marcy: [16:08] It's so pervasive. Everyone thinks that subtle colors aren't really that bad. When you have a foreground color and a background color, you can calculate the ratio, the difference between them, and that is color contrast. That's how we calculate it.

[16:28] So many sites, maybe the ratio is pretty close. The standard is 4.5 to 1.0for regular, non-bold text. If you have headlines that are bigger or bolder, only 3.0to 1.0is the requirement. Say, it's a headline and it's 2.8 to 1.0If everybody around the world is like, "Eh, it's close enough," when you run that automated test, every site is going to fail and the...

Kent: [17:00] That is an indication that there are people who won't be able to see any of those things.

Marcy: [17:05] Exactly. I was going to say, those are the numbers, but the motivation for all of that is that if you have low vision, like maybe you're not completely blind, but you can see some things on the screen, if there isn't enough contrast, you just can't see stuff.

[17:24] Maybe you aren't low vision. Maybe you just have a crappy projector or you're outside in the sun. If something doesn't have enough contrast, you really can't see what's going on on the screen. That one's really important. It's one that just gets shoved aside because it doesn't seem that important, but it's so common.

[17:45] There's other really basic things like image alt text. If an image is useful content, it should have an alt attribute with some text describing what's in it. An example from a blind friend of mine who works at Facebook, he was working on developing their automated alt text algorithm.

[18:06] He was describing his experience before they added that feature was like, "Yeah, all the people in my news feed are talking about their long-lost love, and I didn't know that it was a picture of a pepperoni pizza. What are they talking about?" You could be left out of the joke. Maybe it's a meme, or something in a slide presentation on the web that, if you don't know what's in the content, that's how you can add that metadata, through HTML, using the alt attribute.

[18:38] If it's not a useful image, and it's just decorative, then you add alt, and just have it be an empty value. These are common accessibility things that a good developer checklist can help you cover the top 10. That'll make a big impact.

[18:54] If you can just try and nail those top things, and I'll try to round up a resource for you to include in the notes.

Kent: [19:00] That'd be great.

Marcy: [19:02] There's some great basics that feel like superpowers. When you add all this stuff to an app, it just becomes so much more useful for everyone, whether or not they're blind and using a screen reader. Having good keyboard mechanics, making sure all of your components work only with the keyboard.

[19:23] When your developer elbow flares up, or maybe you have a baby in one arm, or a cup of coffee in one hand. Being able to navigate with only the keyboard is awesome, and there's lots of people who need that, that can't use a mouse, for whatever reason.

Kent: [19:40] Totally. Lots of us developers get really used to and comfortable with the keyboard. I always appreciate it when an application is keyboard accessible.

Marcy: [19:51] Me, too.

Kent: [19:54] That's awesome. What are some of the things that are important for accessibility, but can't be automated?

Marcy: [20:01] The one I mentioned earlier about error validation message content. Alt text can be similar. I think it's getting a little better. Like I mentioned, Facebook is automating their alt text. With machine learning, maybe we can automate what's in an image.

[20:21] If you don't have that at your disposal, or maybe it's not accurate enough yet, having an actual human review, "Is that photo of Kent C. Dodds, or did we forget to update the alt code, and that's actually a picture of Ryan Florence?"

Kent: [20:37] We get confused all the time.

Marcy: [20:42] The things that are more nuanced, for human review. There's web standards or guidelines for a lot of this stuff from the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, or WCAG, if you've heard of that. There is a new revision, WCAG 2.1, that just came out.

[21:03] In WCAG 2.1, which is a new version of the guidelines, they've added items to help make websites and web applications stronger on mobile devices, people with cognitive disabilities, and I think low vision. I think those are the three that they really tried to add.

[21:22] WCAG 2.1 encompasses all of everything that was in WCAG 2.0which is a lot of the stuff that we can automate, but many of the items that they've added now for low vision and cognitive disabilities just can't be automated.

[21:36] There's no hooks for automated tooling to really dig into. That's a really new area of automated testing. I think it'll get better over time. Some of that stuff is just so nuanced, and requires a human to review. I'll have to pull up the whole list to refresh my memory.

Kent: [21:57] Not just a human, it's got to be somebody who understands accessibility and knows what will work and what won't work for people who are using assistive technologies, or are in a situation where they need additional help in using the app.

Marcy: [22:15] There's another one that we've added some workarounds. What you made me think of was, if someone with a disability is an accessibility tester, and they're trying to test for the defect that would prevent them from using the site, sometimes that's really difficult, if you can't see the screen.

[22:35] Say, you have a whole region of your web application that has display none on it, because maybe it's an inactive tab panel, or some piece of content that, maybe it's a modal window that gets opened when you hit a button or something.

[22:51] For our blind testers at DQ, they were missing whole sections of content. We had to add a little hook for them to highlight, "Oh, this is a hidden region," so they know that it even exists. There's some ways that tooling can work around that at least guide the tester to the right part of the application to even know that it exists.

[23:15] It's difficult to do full accessibility testing in a hidden region, because usually, if it's a mega menu or some inactive component, you're just stashing it in its broken state, and it's fine because no one's looking at it. Then, it gets crafted into the perfect component when you hit the button and open it, or whatever.

[23:39] We try to make tools that help that testing experience, even when we can't fully automate all of it.

Kent: [23:46] You have tools for both automated and manual testing, just to help with both sides?

Marcy: [23:52] Yeah, because at the end of the day, we want to enable people to do better testing, because that's the end goal. Sometimes, our hands are tied with web technologies in that there just aren't APIs sometimes.

[24:05] Another example is, if you have a div with a click event on it. You mentioned divs for labels. Another anti-pattern I see a lot with JavaScript is that you can put a click event on anything, but a div is not keyboard accessible.

[24:25] Unless you're using an inline JavaScript handler, like the old school way, an onClick, directly in line, and it's still there in your HTML after it gets rendered, there's no standard cross-browser API for detecting those JavaScript events.

[24:39] Especially if they're bound in memory with jQuery, Angular, or anything like that, or if you have event delegation. That's how React's event system works, is that they're looking at the top level, that way, they can flow events through a synthetic event, if I'm understanding it correctly.

[25:05] That way, if you remove a node or something, you don't get a memory leak when you remove that DOM node, but the event is still attached. It's like, "OK, cool." That's a great system, but if you put a click event on a div, and you're not using a linter or something to point out that that's a problem to you, you'll ship it, and run axe on it.

[25:28] After it's been rendered, there's really no standard way of detecting those events. That's one that I really wish we could solve.

Kent: [25:37] I feel like the browser, I don't even know how the browser could know that, because React is doing event delegation. I'm glad that you brought that up, though, because I almost forgot to mention, there are linters for this. At least, there's one for React.

[25:53] It's ESLint plugin JSX A11Y, or something like that, A11Y JSX, one of those. Just google it. You'll find it. That one's fantastic. I strongly recommend not disabling any of those rules, because they all are good. People should totally use those. I expect that there are similar tools for template-based frameworks, as well.

[26:21] You're right. Tools like axe-core can't really detect that. There's no browser hooks or framework-specific hooks for knowing. Actually, I wonder if you could write a framework adapter or something to...

Marcy: [26:36] You could. There's different areas in your workflow that this problem could be solved. In the linting phase, and React A11Y, which came before that ESLint tool, I think Ryan Florence wrote it. It has a warning thing that'll say, "Hey, you put a click event on a div."

[26:59] You could do it in that scenario. Linting in a framework, like in your IDE, but there are some caveats there. You have to have CSS applied. You do want to do the testing in the rendered environment that you get the clearest picture of what a user would experience.

[27:18] Linting, while it will catch some things, and I think there's some experimentation being done to get axe-core and that ESLint tool to play together. It's going to be a limited subset of what axe-core is running, because you need that rendered environment.

[27:37] There's false positives that can come from, "Oh, my form looks fine in raw JSX or whatever, HTML, but my CSS is putting display:none on the labels, so those become ineffective," basically. You need that whole picture.

[27:55] It's nice to use a variety of tools. You can catch some things in linting, do some testing in your automated test suite that runs it with Selenium, Cypress, or whatever your automated tools are. You can catch it in various places.

[28:12] There's useful tools for all of those things. I think the message should be that there's a variety of tools, and we should be using more than one.

Kent: [28:24] I think that's a great way to wrap up. It shouldn't just be automated tools, either. It's not possible to get 100 percent accessibility test coverage on an application.

[28:39] Getting somebody on your team or at your company to study up on accessibility, or hiring somebody from DQ to come give you an audit, I think, is really the only way to be as certain as possible that your application is usable by people who are using assistive technologies, and need extra help in using the application.

[29:03] Just two last things really quick, Marcy. If you could give everybody who's watching some homework of something to do for their applications, what would you have them do?

Marcy: [29:17] I'm glad you asked, because I was going to say it, anyway. The first thing I would do is ditch your mouse for a day out of week. Go mouse-free Mondays, mouse-free Fridays, or whatever, and try to navigate whatever you're building without a mouse or a track pad, and no cheating.

[29:38] You'll quickly find that there might be areas that you missed. That gets into some of the areas that we can't necessarily automate. If you've got some controls that are just built with divs, you can't reach them with the keyboard, neither can an automated tool.

[29:56] One workflow hack that I really like for that is if you use something like Selenium web driver, you could write some tests that, say, you're opening a modal window. Go to try open it with a key event. Selenium will scream at you if you put a key event on an element that is not focusable.

[30:16] It's doing some accessibility automation for you, just by trying to send it key events. You can send it an enter key with the spacebar or whatever, if you're on the button that triggers that open. You can do some validation with your tooling.

[30:34] Even just the manual, like "Tab through the page. Can you do everything without a mouse?" That would be so awesome. That gets so many keyboard users, screen reader users. Keyboards and screen readers, you can't really...I don't know. The screen reader is made effective by good keyboard support.

[30:57] The other thing I would recommend is get a browser extension to just run something like axe. There are other tools, as well. If you just make it part of your workflow so you're doing it with each pull request or whatever cadence works for you, try and make it a regular thing. Get other people on board.

[31:19] Specialists are great because they can help you learn areas that you don't know about, but really, to be the most effective, it's everyone's responsibility, including your managers and your QA people. It takes a village.

[31:34] If you start small and just...You're not going to fix it all overnight, especially if you're new to it. Start with that developer checklist. Just get that low-hanging fruit. Those things are often really easy to fix. You can get easy wins before you have to tackle the more complex issues.

Kent: [31:54] By the time you get to those more complex things...I don't know about anybody else. Actually, you mentioned this earlier. When I'm fixing accessibility, I feel like a superpower. I'm like, "Yeah! This is so awesome!"

[32:07] When I get to those more complex things, I'm already jazzed about how much more accessible my app is. I'm excited to tackle those bigger things.

Marcy: [32:17] I guess one last thing would be if you do some of that low-hanging fruit -- each team member can be responsible for things like form labels and alt text -- that frees up your specialists or your hired guns to not have to focus on those super-little basics.

[32:33] They can spend their energy working through accessible user experience, making sure your amazing, groundbreaking custom component still works for someone with a disability. You free them up for the more complex stuff, the more complicated Web application things.

[32:52] It can get very complicated. Especially, it's a lot of client-side logic, a lot of notifications happening. Sometimes, there isn't an established pattern for whatever you're creating.

[33:06] You might need some human power to think through some of those decisions. You can use a browser extension or an automated library to get that easy stuff out of the way.

Kent: [33:18] That's super. One last thing. Is there anything or anybody that you would like to give a particular shout-out to? On any topic. Doesn't have to be related to this. This is your opportunity to push something in society that you want to push or anything that you want to shout out.

Marcy: [33:39] Yeah. I guess top of mind, just in my personal life, is that I'd love to give a shout-out to Deque and the folks that are working there very hard. They keep that machine going.

[33:51] Wilco Fiers is my colleague. He stepped up as product owner for axe-core and all of the stuff around it. He's just doing a really exceptional job. They've been there while I am out on my sabbatical. I just want to say thanks.

Kent: [34:09] That's great. Thank you very much, Marcy. This was so awesome, to have you on here. I hope it's helpful for everybody who watches the recording.

Marcy: [34:17] Thanks for having me, Kent.

Kent: [34:20] Bye, everybody.

recommend not disabling any of

That's super. One last thing. Is

fields.title: Craftmanship with Kent Beck
fields.summary: Kent is Original signer of the Agile Manifesto, Author of the Extreme Programming book series, rediscoverer of Test-Driven Development, and inspiring Keynote Speaker.
fields.transcript:
Kent C. Dodds: [00:02] Hey, everybody. It's Kent C. Dodds again. I'm here joined by another Kent. This is wild. We have Kent Beck. Say hi, Kent.

Kent Beck: [00:10] Hi, Kent...

Kent C. Dodds: [00:10] [laughs]

Kent Beck: [00:11] to you and to everybody else.

Kent C. Dodds: [00:14] This is an honor and a privilege, to be chatting with Kent Beck, who's probably been doing software for just about as long as I've been alive. [laughs] I'm pretty young, Kent.

Kent Beck: [00:25] Probably longer.

Kent C. Dodds: [00:25] [laughs] I turn 30 in seven days.

Kent Beck: [00:30] [laughs] Yeah.

Kent C. Dodds: [00:33] [laughs]

Kent Beck: [00:34] I was already a professional programmer by the time you were born.

Kent C. Dodds: [00:38] Oh, snap. Looking forward to the insights you can give us from your wealth of experience. Kent, I'm sure a lot of people have heard your name at least, but maybe not everybody watching. Lots of people who are watching are probably newer to the industry.

[00:54] Could you give us an intro to who you are, why you care so much about software, and just a couple minutes on intro for you?

Kent Beck: [01:05] Sure. Why I care so much is some sort of pathology. A few years ago, I was able to boil down my personal mission to helping geeks feel safe in the world. What I noticed is there were a lot of times when I felt anxious when I was actually safe and there were other times when I didn't feel anxious when I wasn't safe.

[01:30] That comes down to personal growth things, but also software testing. If the software really is ready to go and you feel bad, then that's not good. If the software's not ready to go and you feel good, then that's not good. Testing is a way of validating your feelings one way or another.

[01:53] Something like test-driven development, where you write tests piecemeal just in advance of implementing functionality, is also a way of controlling anxiety, which I had increasing amounts of as I gained experience.

[02:12] Something like software patterns is the idea that by looking at the repeating situations that we run into as programmers, we can do a better job of isolating the thinking about the tradeoffs involved and figuring out what it is that we want to do the next time we run into a similar situation.

[02:33] Extreme programming is also connected in the sense that it's a social structure and a way of structuring the social interactions that are necessary for software development. That's especially useful for people for whom structuring social interactions is a source of anxiety.

[03:02] All around, everything that I've done is somewhere, somehow on that theme. The latest things that I've been working on are thinking about how software development changes as the source of risk changes.

[03:19] At first, in a project, nobody cares. That's your biggest source of risk. Eventually, you run across something that people really care about. Being able to scale that is a risk.

[03:29] Once you've scaled it, being able to sustain that as a business and the profitability funding the next round of innovations, that becomes the risk. The rules of the game change a lot. This is the 3X Explore, Expand, Extract idea.

[03:47] Just a couple weeks ago, I came across an alternative to test-driven development. A workshop I was holding in Oslo, Oddmund Strømme came up with a variant on the TDD workflow that I had never tried before. It sounded so crazy and stupid, I just had to try it.

[04:09] That's test, commit, revert, where if the tests pass, you automatically commit whatever you're working on. If the tests fail, you automatically revert whatever changes you made from the last time the tests passed.

[04:26] I thought this would just be annoying as all get-out. It is very, very early in this and might run into all kinds of problems, but it shouldn't have worked at all. It does work at all. I'm really curious how that's going to play out.

Kent C. Dodds: [04:42] That's super-interesting.

Kent Beck: [04:44] Does that answer your question?

Kent C. Dodds: [04:46] Yeah. Test, commit, revert. That's interesting. When we say commit and revert, are we talking Git-specific terms?

Kent Beck: [04:54] No. Git, for some reason, calls revert reset. That's OK. There's two levels it. There's the personal level of TCR, which is literally -- this is the oddest feeling -- you can try and break your code. It will just repair itself.

[05:16] You can change expected values. You can change some logic. You save the code. Boom. Whatever changes you made disappear. It goes back to wherever things were working before.

[05:35] That's part of a larger goal, which is how could you scale software collaboration. I've become disenchanted with the scaling limits of the pull request code review model and wondering what would happen if you had micro-commits instead.

[05:54] This is the idea of, I call it limbo because the question is "How low can you go? How small can you make commits?" Theoretically, there's no limit to how far you push that.

[06:05] I'm really curious what happens if you use a workflow like test, commit, revert along with collaboration and push changes all the way to production. Everybody live, all the time. There's lots of reasons why that's impossible. Fixing those reasons could be really, really interesting.

Kent C. Dodds: [06:28] Absolutely. I've seen a lot of benefits to the continuous integration, continuous delivery idea. If we can nail that, make that...

Kent Beck: [06:40] I'm that person who jumps ahead and says, "Well, what if we take those trends?" If you look at continuous integration, we're integrating more often now than we were a year ago. A year ago, we were integrating more often than a year before.

[06:56] What if you just take that out 10 years or 15 years? What if you're integrating minutes or seconds instead of integrating in hours and days? Clearly, that won't work, but if you can eliminate the reasons why it won't work, then I think you have...

[07:18] I don't know what you unlock in terms of us being able to interact as software developers, but I'm really curious to find out.

Kent C. Dodds: [07:27] It's really interesting how solving today's problems can unlock things that we didn't know would be possible when those problems were no longer problems.

Kent Beck: [07:37] We just get so used to our current constraints that we accept them as laws of nature and not just this is a box that I built, that I put myself in. I can choose to knock down the walls. Then the world's a different place. Then I'm in a bigger box, but that's OK. That process is never going to end.

Kent C. Dodds: [08:00] Hey, Kent. I want to ask you about something that you said on Stack Overflow back in 2008. You ready for...

Kent Beck: [08:07] Uh oh. Have a great one.

Kent C. Dodds: [08:08] 2008 Kent Beck coming to haunt you?

Kent Beck: [08:11] Go ahead.

Kent C. Dodds: [08:12] It was just in an answer. You said the phrase, "I pay...I get paid to..." Sorry. Let me start that over. "I get paid for code that works, not for tests."

Kent Beck: [08:22] That's correct.

Kent C. Dodds: [08:24] I think that touches on something I think is really important, is the practicality of testing. We don't live in a utopia. We need to apply these principles in a pragmatic way, where often we get very...

[08:40] Yes, test everything. It's so important. Confidence and workflows and things. At the end of the day, we're paid for the product and for the code, not the test. Can you expound on that idea a little bit more?

Kent Beck: [08:52] I'm going to backpedal in a second, but I'll start by defending myself. I was responding to people who were saying, "Everything should be tested absolutely perfectly all the time, or else you're a bad person. Naughty, naughty program." I don't believe that. It doesn't make sense.

[09:17] Since then, I would have more sophisticated vocabulary for discussing when that kind of attitude makes more sense and when it makes less sense. In early exploration, if you're just going to throw the code away, there's no downside to making mistakes. Then writing tests has progressively less value.

[09:41] If you tilt the tradeoffs the other way, testing's going to have more and more value. I think that's the defense, the context in which I said that.

[09:56] It is true that one of my early experiences on Wall Street was explaining my careful, craftsman-like approach to software development and how I would deliver a comprehensive test suite and being asked, "Well, how much sooner could you give it to me if you didn't write the tests?"

[10:18] I thought, it's a different set of trade-offs. My snarky answer was, "Well, it'd take me two more weeks." Where I think now is, mostly the value of software is not the value it delivers today. It's the value that it can deliver tomorrow. It's the option value that's embedded in it.

[10:41] Tests can enhance or inhibit that option value. I've seen tests in this strict mocking style that make it extremely difficult to change a single line of code. Those reduce the option value. They're, strictly speaking, a lose. It's just like looking at the program with the world's worst syntax.

[11:06] There are other tests that increase the option value of software. I think that I'm also asked to deliver today, but I'm asked to deliver something unpredictable and different tomorrow, and [inaudible] I built. Then they're delivering economic value today.

[11:30] I would say I went too far in saying, "Oh, I get paid for code, not tests." I don't think I even get paid for code. I think I get paid for options for tomorrow's code, to a larger degree. Tests may or may not enhance that.

Kent C. Dodds: [11:48] That's a perfect response. That's a really good point, that sure, you could say to that businessperson, "Yeah, I could get it to you sooner, but you won't be able to do anything with it when it's done."

[12:01] This is the way that it is. This is the way. It will always work this way, all the bugs. If you want me to go in and fix it, I'll have to spend a whole month redesigning it, rearchitecting it, and testing it, so that I can enhance it in the future.

Kent Beck: [12:18] Which may be the appropriate trade-off. There are projects with a certain risk profile where that's the right thing to do, is to give somebody something in a week that'll take a month to re-implement the right way.

[12:32] There are other projects where the right thing to do is to refuse to deliver something in a week, and only deliver the thing that you can do in a month. Now, even in those cases, I'm still going to a deliver a quarter of that project after a week, because I'm not smart enough to integrate everything all at once.

[12:53] To deliver something in a week, I'm going to have to deliver something every day. To deliver something every day, I'm going to have to deliver something every hour, and, and, and, down to the smallest increments that I can imagine.

[13:07] That trade-off about testing, it's a genuine trade-off. There's times to push in different directions. I think it's really worth thinking about which tests enhance optionality, and which style of testing inhibit optionality.

Kent C. Dodds: [13:26] Let's talk about that a little bit. Are you referring to certain workflows specifically, or certain types of testing? Is TDD, does that have any influence in here, or is it more general than that?

Kent Beck: [13:44] The one that I know is painful, this is an argument against unit testing. The more tests I write, the harder it is to refactor. I think, "Well, that's not my experience, so what's going on with that?" Then I see test suites where I think, [shudders] "Of course, this makes it harder to refactor."

[14:05] Where a test says, create these objects. Now, here's the expectation. First, I'm going to send this message to that object, which is going to send this message to that object, which is with these parameters, which will send this message to that object with these parameters.

[14:24] That's just source code, but with a horrific syntax. I can have this thousand-line test that mimics single stepping in a debugger through five layers of message text. Yeah, now, if I want to change anything about the implementation, I have to change the source code and these tests.

[14:53] These tests are horrifically difficult to understand. Those tests, what I would call the strict mock style, inhibit optionality. It's absolutely duplicate information of what's in the source code, and it makes it really hard to refactor.

[15:11] That's why I say that most testing problems are design problems, they aren't testing problems. If I'm having trouble testing something, it's because there's something about the design that I don't understand well enough.

[15:25] Sometimes, I can step back, and I think, "What kind of design would I need in order to be able to test this?" I come up with the idea, and then both implementation and testing become easier. Sometimes, I can't think of how to do it, and then I have to decide, do I go ahead, and I do abandon this, or defer this line of development? Does that answer your question?

Kent C. Dodds: [15:53] That answers the question well. I think that lots of the problem that I see in tests like that is that they're testing implementation detail. People are thinking about code coverage. What I try to tell people is, think more about use case coverage.

[16:11] You're more interested in the specific use cases that this code is supposed to support. When you think about use cases, you can't think about implementation, because that's not part of my use case.

Kent Beck: [16:23] I'm not sure, code coverage is useful to me personally, just to find out what's covered and what's not. Sometimes, it delivers surprises. If it delivers surprises, those are questions that I need to answer. "Oh, I thought that was covered, and it's not. Why not? What test am I missing? What about my workflow would have covered this?"

[16:55] More's not better. A hundred, except by construction, is not a useful goal. One of, my surprise most-viewed tweet of all time was, "Being proud of 100 percent test coverage is like being proud of reading every word in the newspaper."

Kent C. Dodds: [17:19] I remember seeing that one.

Kent Beck: [17:23] Then it blew up, and whatever. Somehow, people would like this to be a simpler problem than it. They would like to take insight, creativity, and careful thought. Sometimes, problems just have to stew for a while, months or years before you get it. They like to take that out and replace it with more coverage is better. No, not necessarily.

[17:57] Sometimes, you reframe a problem. You come up with a new abstraction for a problem, and the sources of the defects disappear just because now you have a declared expression of something that was imperative and had data aliasing problems. Now, all that just goes away.

[18:22] Where's that show up on code coverage? It probably doesn't, but your life as a programmer just got vastly better. The lives of the users of the software just got vastly better because a whole class of bugs disappeared. The optionality of the software increased dramatically so the people who care about the dollars and cents about the software just increased dramatically.

[18:45] It had absolutely nothing to do with code coverage. It wasn't an incremental state step change. It was somebody being on vacation, in the shower, swimming in the ocean, or something going, "Oh, I see! This computation is really a calculus of the things and the other things. And if we have these operators, then the whole thing becomes simple."

Kent C. Dodds: [19:10] That's a really interesting point. I feel like lots of people think, "If I just code more, I'll get better at coding." That is true. There's no shortcut to experience, but I think a lot of coding is exercising your brain and keeping your brain healthy.

[19:25] That does not necessarily involve coding 100 percent of the time. You need to take time away and do things for your brain that are healthy for your brain, develop your brain.

Kent Beck: [19:35] I strive for a rested, prepared mind. Those two things sometimes come into conflict, and that's fine. The preparation is gathering lots and lots of raw experiences. The "rested" is going and doing other things. We talked about music. Art's another hobby of mine and talking to people who aren't in tech.

[20:01] I have lots of friends who are in music and art and in different kind of worlds. I think that all of that stuff makes me better prepared for those big jumps forward.

Kent C. Dodds: [20:16] Absolutely. We've just got a couple minutes left here, Kent. I wanted to ask you about test-driven development. Maybe you can correct me, but you rediscovered test-driven development. Is that right?

Kent Beck: [20:29] Yeah, that's how I would put it. It's as old as programming. It was used on the software for Project Mercury in the early '60s. When I started talking about TDD, older programmers at that time, 60-something programmers at that time, would come up to me and say, "We always worked this way. Why don't people do it?"

[20:54] I can't claim to have invented it. I will claim to have rediscovered it in my own quirky way. It was an idea that was out there, this idea of double-checking your software. There's a principle involved, double-checking software by expressing illustrative examples.

Kent C. Dodds: [21:21] The nice thing about this test first is those illustrative examples are use cases, not implementation details. Just by design, test-driven development helps you build tests that are free of implementation details, right?

Kent Beck: [21:36] It's certainly easier to write tests from the outside ignoring the inside if you don't have an inside yet. You're just thinking in terms of the API, so writing tests for purely functional code is easier than writing tests for imperative code, where you've got this big ball of state that has to be set up just so before you get started. It puts my laziness to work. Those are going to be the easiest tests for me to write.

Kent C. Dodds: [22:12] There was one time I was running with test-driven development on some purely functional code that I was having a blast with. It was awesome. When I finished, the code that I had was this monstrosity of the worst code I've ever written. I never want to touch that code ever again.

[22:32] [laughs] I believe that the reason is that I kind of skipped the refactor step [laughs] where I go back and, "Let's refactor this, make this continue to pass the test that we've written, but make it something that we want to work in."

[22:48] Can you talk a little bit about the importance of the steps and the workflow and how that helps you design good software?

Kent Beck: [22:55] Sure. I hesitate because this is a, I'm going to say, natural, but maybe it's a developed habit. It certainly works with my tendency towards OCD. I like things to be quite tidy. Part of my satisfaction in development is seeing new functionality work, but part of my satisfaction in development is understanding how things work.

[23:31] When I understand how things work better, "Oh, these two things that looked very different are actually expressions of the same computation," now I have an opportunity. If I can unify those concepts in the code, so that there's one concept in my mind and one concept in the code, I feel better. I cement my understanding of how complicated the world really is.

[24:01] I think programmers panic. They think there's not going to be enough time. If you implement a feature, your only choice is to [speaking quickly] implement the next feature as quickly as you possibly can. [breathing] Simple exercise, in through the nose, out through the mouth. [breathing]

[24:27] I implemented a feature, "Ah, OK. Well, maybe I should get up and get a drink of water. Or maybe the two of us working together ought to go drink a coffee. How'd that go? How could we do it better? Is there a latent symmetry in the code, things that are similar that currently look different, but we think maybe they could be the same? Let's go explore that."

[24:57] That's one part of the trade-off, in economics terms, to improve the optionality of the code. The other part of the trade-off is there is a lot to do, but there's always more to do than there is code. Panicking is not going to change that.

[25:16] Even if we find the right balance between new functionality and cleaning up old functionality, there's still going to be too much to do. I'm not going to change that. Part of the discipline of development in this style is not panicking. It's waiting for those moments when we can simplify the code. If we don't know how to simplify the code, having the discipline to make the problem worse sometimes.

[25:49] Maybe that bigger ball of mud is what's going to give us the insight to separate parts of it out and say, "OK, but this part really, this can be simple, and the rest of it maybe is still complicated." At Tektronix, the phrase was "a sense of enlightened urgency."

[26:12] Urgency, "we gotta get some stuff done," but enlightened in understanding that the big jumps forward are not going to come from being five percent faster at typing. Not that being good at typing is bad. One of the exercises that I talk about is learning how to touch type, but that's not going to be a big jump forward. It's a really useful skill.

Kent C. Dodds: [26:43] I really like that idea of acknowledging that there's an insatiable desire for getting more things done and acknowledging the fact that it is insatiable. Finishing this feature and working on the next one, there is no end to this. There's no finish line...

[27:02] [crosstalk]

Kent C. Dodds: [27:02] so there's no reason to keep going at breakneck speed. Take a break.

Kent Beck: [27:08] Right. The fact that there's more and more things that people want the software to do is good news. The more intense that desire, the greater potential energy there is in the software. That's all good news. If you have established software and it's out there doing a good job, and then all of a sudden people start complaining about it, that's bad news.

[27:33] That means you messed something up out of something with a lot of value. If you have software, it's starting to pick up steam, and people are desperate for it to do new and better things, that's fantastic. Two weeks ago nobody cared at all, which is a [laughs] much harder problem to solve.

Kent C. Dodds: [27:54] It also occurred to me that taking those breaks and crafting your software well, there's a reason we want to do that. We are motivated to get things out quicker. There's a big motivation there, and it's reasonable. We're not taking breaks just because we like to take breaks. We're not crafting software better just because we like to craft software better.

[28:18] It's because, ultimately, it will help us actually move faster. If we take time to think about the design patterns we're implementing and things, ultimately, we'll have a better time. We won't have as many problems with shipping broken code, and we'll be able to move faster at the end of the day.

Kent Beck: [28:39] The 3X model, this explore, expand, extract, was a big step forward for me thinking about these kinds of trade-offs. There are times when shipping a feature that works 80 percent of the time to find out if anybody cares at all is a really valuable thing to do.

[28:59] There are other times, like financial transactions, where shipping a feature that works 80 percent of the time is utterly and completely irresponsible, unethical, and business suicide. That same behavior can be really useful and can be completely irresponsible.

[29:27] I need to know where I am on that curve of, "Am I battling the forces of indifference or am I battling the forces of sustainability and entropy?" Depending on where I am, I should behave very, very differently.

Kent C. Dodds: [29:49] I think that's a great insight. We're just about wrapped up. I have two questions to ask at the very end here. The first is what is our homework? If you could give people watching one thing to do to improve their software craftsmanship or their testability of their code or their tests, what would that be?

Kent Beck: [30:06] A simple exercise. Take something that you implemented recently and implement it again. You know how big it is. You know what's involved. There aren't going to be huge domain surprises. What that does is encourages you to focus on your workflow, on your tooling, on the design, on the way you slice the salami and the order in which you eat the slices. You play with all that stuff when you're re-implementing something.

[30:43] It seems, "Hey, I already have the software. It already works. Why would I do it?" You do it not to get the software, but to really focus on the process of getting the software. In everybody's development workflow, there are huge leaks.

[31:01] There's places where you're wasting time, repetitive motion that if you could get rid of those things, you would just be more effective. Taking domain surprises out of that equation is a way to really focus on that.

Kent C. Dodds: [31:16] I think that's fantastic advice. I always chuckle when somebody says, "We built this part of our app in one framework. Then, after that, we went and built it in this other framework. This one was so much better for so many reasons. This one must be better."

[31:30] I think there was a difference between the developers, like your brain at this point and your brain at this point. Being able to see the end from the beginning is a huge benefit in developing software. I think that's great advice.

[31:47] Last question. Is there anyone or anything that you would like to give a particular shout-out to? Doesn't have to be related to testing or even software. It could just be anything.

Kent Beck: [31:55] I'm going to go with my impulse, which is I think looking at the way that President Obama conducted his time in office has tremendous lessons for how I want to live my life.

[32:15] The kind of people that I want to be connected to, the way that I communicate, the level of empathy, the dignity. I think those are all things that I would like to emulate. Big shout-out to Barack.

Kent C. Dodds: [32:35] Thank you, President Obama. Thank you so much, Kent. That was an awesome chat. I think people will really benefit from this time that we had together.

Kent Beck: [32:44] Hope so.

Kent C. Dodds: [32:47] We're signing out. Thanks, everybody.

Kent Beck: [32:50] Bye-bye.

Kent C. Dodds: [32:50] Bye.

00:24:57,140 --> 00:25:02,030
That's one part of the trade-off, 

641

642

643

644

645

646

651

It seems, "Hey, I already have

Why would I do it?" You do it

Taking domain surprises out of

00:31:16,610 --> 00:31:18,190
I think that's fantastic advice.

00:31:18,190 --> 00:31:20,430
I always chuckle when somebody

fields.title: Ministry of testing with Rosie Sherry
fields.summary: Rosie is a founder of Software Testing Club, she loves all things communities, bootstrapping and unschooling.

fields.title: Practical testing with Wes Bos and Scott Tolinski
fields.summary:
Wes is a full stack web developer and designer from Hamilton, Canada. He loves to share what he knows through his training products, teaching and speaking at conferences.

Scott Tolinski is the creator of Level Up Tutorials where he has released thousands of free and premium web development tutorials. Scott also is the co-host of the popular web development podcast Syntax. In his free time Scott is a dedicated Bboy (breakdancer) & enjoys pushing himself athletically through dance, working out and snowboarding.
fields.transcript:
Kent Dodds: [00:00] Hey, everybody. It is Kent C. Dodds, here, and I'm with the Syntax crew. [laughs] We have Wes Bos and Scott Tolinski. We're going to be talking about testing, of course. Why don't we get a quick intro to Wes and Scott here.

[00:14] Scott, could you intro yourself? Just tell us who you are, what you are all about with testing in particular or just in general with software, what do you care about and that stuff.

Scott Tolinski: [00:24] Sure. Hey, my name is Scott Tolinski, I'm a Full Stack Developer from Denver, Colorado. I run and created Level Up tutorials, which is a large resource for free web development tutorials on YouTube and through premium tutorials on the website.

[00:39] Testing, for me, has always been very important and crucial for my business to stay up and running. For instance, I elected to essentially create my own platform because of all my custom needs for e-commerce and subscription-based everything.

[00:53] Everything on my site is totally custom, and because of that, making sure everything is fully tested has always been an extremely important aspect of my business and my career.

[01:04] I'm really interested in making really modern, good-looking web applications. Because of that, testing has always been one of those things that needs to happen to make those goals come true.

Kent: [01:18] Sweet. Thanks, Scott. Wes, can you give us an intro to yourself?

Wes Bos: [01:22] Yeah. My name's Wes. I'm a Full Stack Developer, I'm also a course creator. I make stuff, primarily revolving around JavaScript. Probably the most popular one is one called javascript30.com, but you can check them all out at wesbos.com.

[01:36] I'm really interested in testing because I run this massive course platform with hundreds of thousands people taking my courses. At the end of the day, it's mostly just me running this entire thing, and I've always been a huge proponent of just trying to automate as much as I possibly can behind this.

[01:59] Because, while I am starting to reach out into having other people help me work on the course platform, it's really important to me that I have things in place that are going to catch the inevitable human error that I will inject into the code base. Big fan of having fail safes as well as just having not people do stuff.

Kent: [02:25] Nice. We got to stop all that human error injecting, man.

Scott: [02:31] Inevitable human error is the key.

Wes: [02:33] [laughs]

Kent: [02:35] Yeah, seriously. I think it's pretty clear why both of you care about testing so much. You only have so much time in a day.

[02:43] Wait, that almost sounds, I don't know, like some people could find that as contradictory. It's like, well, so, you only have so much time, so why do you spend so much of your time writing code that no users are ever going to run or see or anything? Why don't you just focus on product work?

[03:00] Why does testing matter? How does testing save you time?

Scott: [03:03] For me, personally, I don't like to do the same thing twice, especially if it's up and working. The last thing I want to do is to have something that works entirely, make a change somewhere else, and then have to go back and redo work.

[03:14] Not only that, but the amount of time debugging and finding problems and having your users find those problems. All of that stuff is just big hamper to productivity in general and the amount of time that you could spend creating things, because you're spending time worrying about stuff that is already working at one point and now no longer working.

[03:37] For me, it seems contradictory, I totally agree. The amount of time up front spent is going to save you so much time down the line and that's just hugely important.

Wes: [03:47] Because I don't want to bring out my laptop on the weekend, meaning that, I don't want to deploy stuff to, like my entire business runs on this, my entire income is dependent on this thing actually running. If I'm picking apples with my kids in an orchard and I take out my phone and I see that everything is broken, that's a bummer. It sucks when that kind of thing happens.

[04:09] I think what I mean by that is I want to have confidence in my code knowing that I'm probably not going to have to run home from apple picking or something like that, because everything has catastrophically failed, or I'm not making any money today because something has gone down.

[04:30] Like Scott says, being able to refactor parts of your application, it's so important that you have that confidence. I think that's the key word here, is just having confidence in the code that you write that things aren't going to go bust.

Kent: [04:42] Totally. Confidence is the biggest thing for me as well. I know a lot of people are really into the design aspects of testing, like if you use test first principles or test-driven development, that the end results, you can wind up with a better designed code. I'm mostly interested in the confidence as well.

[05:03] Do either of you do much test-driven development or test designed code?

Scott: [05:08] It depends, for me. My interface code is largely, tests are written after the fact. I'll write the interface, I'll make everything work and then I'll write the tests. If I'm writing a lot of my API stuff, my server side functionality, almost always, I'll do test-driven [inaudible] , that test-driven development, or those aspects.

[05:26] For some reason it makes more sense to me if I'm running a server side function that's going off and doing this or that, it makes more sense for me to do that as like a TDD approach rather than the interface stuff, where I may be tweaking it, and I'm tweaking it, and I'm tweaking it before it's done.

Wes: [05:42] Yeah, I'm the same way as Scott as well. I think most, I would like to be test-driven, and I probably will catch that bug at some point. Where I'm at right now, especially with my course platform application that's been around for, I think I've had it for like six or seven years so far and I've only added testing into it maybe about the last year or so.

[06:04] Most of that has been, go back and test it and I'm seeing the benefits of maybe writing the test before. Then, in some cases, if I'm writing a React component, I just want to build the thing. I know what it is that I want to do, and then after the fact, I can go back and write some tests to make sure that it does what I want.

Kent: [06:23] Yeah, I'm actually really the same way. Except recently with React testing library, I've found that it's actually now practical to start test driving UI, which it never really felt that way before.

[06:35] I think it was because our tools before were all very implementation detail-focused, but with kind of a tool that allows us to avoid implementation details of our components, it's actually, test driving UI is now possible. In the course, I show how to build a form using test-driven development, like integrating with React Router and a bunch of other things.

[07:00] Scott, I know that you've used React testing library quite a bit. Have you tried test-driven development with that?

Scott: [07:08] Yeah, I tried it. I found myself, because I do a lot of my design phase in browser while I'm coding, I find myself going into a lot of UI components with a less clear idea of what it needs to be. For the most part, that's primarily why I don't, because I end up spending so much time tweaking things, just because simply I don't have all the details worked out before I start ahead of time.

[07:32] I think if I was in a normal environment where maybe I'm given design comps or just a better clear idea of where I'm going at the end of it, then I think that would make a lot more sense for me.

[07:44] I'm definitely sort of a mess around with the code type of person while I'm doing it. That doesn't necessarily lend itself super well to planning out the tests ahead of time. I think it could, it depends on the situation.

Kent: [07:59] That makes total sense. I definitely don't TDD UI a lot, like I was literally just minutes ago working on this UI thing and I don't even have any tests for it yet. Yeah, it's just UI is a special beast.

[08:14] A question that people are constantly asking me about is, how do I know what to test? I think that's probably the biggest challenge that people have with testing.

[08:25] Like, I've got all this code, like, even thinking about that is daunting for a lot of people. Do I test that this, when this button is clicked, it calls this function? Or do I test that, like, this class method is called or do I stub that out? How do you know what to test? What would you say about that, Wes?

Wes: [08:53] For me, I think, when I look back at my code base, it's a good example, I had this massive code base that didn't have any tests in it. Then I started to feel those aches and pains of not having any tests, being able to refactor.

[09:07] At that point, I think about it not in terms of what's the best technical thing to do, but it's like a, how is this [inaudible] going to save my butt when things start to go wrong? The answer to that, for me, is like, just test it as if the user were to be using the website themselves. Or test it as if the...

[09:29] Whatever gets me as close as possible to the actual user using that component, that's kind of where I'm going to focus my efforts on first. Then you can kind of expand out from there as you have more time. Because, let's face it, we don't all have time to get 100 percent code coverage.

Scott: [09:47] Yeah, I feel the same way. For me, it's largely, it's like you said, the stuff that the people are going to be using.

[09:54] For me, I always start at mission critical stuff. If I'm approaching something with no tests, I go to mission critical and I start testing exactly the most important things.

[10:07] If I have a shopping cart, the first thing I do is check to make sure orders go through. Then I check to make sure that the correct things are being added to the cart. Then I make sure that the total's adding up correctly. Either way, it starts at what's mission critical and how are the users going to be using it.

Kent: [10:24] I think that's super key and I'm glad that you both mentioned that.

[10:29] A couple months ago, maybe it was a year ago, I Tweeted something that actually, for the first time, somebody yesterday contradicted me and I was surprised by this. Because I feel like it's not at all controversial. The Tweet is, "The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you."

[10:49] Taking a step back and thinking about, "Hey, if I were to manually test this, how would I go about doing that?" then trying to make your test map to that kind of process.

[10:59] I think really helps me. I never really ask the question of what do I test, what do I not test. I just think about it from the perspective of the user and I would test it how they use it, which I think it's pretty critical.

[11:13] Wes, you mentioned one thing about, you said, not everybody has time to get 100 percent code coverage. I'm curious what you both feel is a good guideline as far as code coverage is concerned. Are you striving for 100 percent or are you cool with 20? [laughs]

Wes: [11:32] That's a good question. I don't even know what I'm at. I do know that the things that make me money and the things that the users use have coverage on them, because those are the two most important things to me.

[11:44] I want people to be able to buy my stuff and want people to have an enjoyable experience viewing the videos, tracking their progress, everything that goes into building a course platform. I feel pretty comfortable having both of those things covered.

[12:01] Then once that's done, then I start to go into the nitty-gritty in terms of like, I have lots of logic into how the coupons work. There's percentage in coupons and there's dollar amount and the coupons can be restrictive based on a course or a specific part of a course. It's very, very complex how the coupons work.

[12:19] Then once I know that the basics works, then I'll go into a bit more nitty-gritty. I don't really have an answer in terms of what percentage is good.

Scott: [12:27] Yeah. I use largely code coverage to look at the pretty graph. That's pretty much it. I don't necessarily trust any of the numbers. I love seeing the green colors in the graph and that's pretty much it.

[12:39] It's the same as Wes, the stuff that drives my business is the stuff that I make sure is covered and I make sure is covered completely. Everything else, like if I'm getting a zero percent coverage on my About Us page I don't really care.

[12:55] [laughs]

Kent: [12:57] Totally. I think that's the real key that people miss about code coverage is that, a one percent code coverage increase over here is not equal to one percent code coverage increase over here.

[13:08] These coverage numbers are, as far as your business goes, different. Yeah, I love that we've kind come to this idea of, focus on the mission critical stuff. Then, as the bugs in the non-mission critical stuff become a bother to you, then, yeah, go ahead and go test them.

[13:32] If there's a bug that you ship, like you said, the About page, it's not a big deal. You go fix it. If that keeps happening, then, yeah, maybe add some tests, mostly because it's annoying, not because it matters all that much.

Scott: [13:45] Yeah, the code coverage graphs are good for Instagram photos.

[13:48] [laughter]

Kent: [13:50] I want to see one of those. Cool. I think we've pretty much covered most of the things that I wanted to talk about in our interview. Is there anything about testing that either one of you want to talk about before we wrap things up?

Wes: [14:05] I think that testing sort of has, especially talking from people who have taken my courses and who maybe are necessarily a couple years into coding. It seems very scary to them, and I think that there's this level of shaming people who don't test their code involved in our industry.

[14:24] Same with the accessibility as well, people sort of just skirt it and not talk about it because I don't want to get called out on this kind of thing.

[14:34] The reality is, like, just give it a shot, just try some stuff out. It doesn't have to be 100 percent code coverage and you don't have to know all of the tools. Just get something up and running and you'll start to get a little bit more comfortable with it. You'll see the benefits of it.

[14:49] There's a reason why people are passionate about it, it's because they've been burned before. Once you can get that in place, I think that you'll start to see why everyone is so passionate about it.

Scott: [15:01] Yeah. I would like to add on, too, on top of that, that writing tests, I feel like, is one of those things that's made me a better developer overall, because code that's easy to test or feels comfortable to test is usually better code.

[15:15] I guess that's maybe a personal journey that I've been on is that, you can use tests not only to insure that your application's running correctly, but you can also use tests to sort of be like a code smell. Is this a good idea the way I'm doing this? Am I able to test this correctly or not? It's a big thing.

[15:34] I 100 percent agree with everything Wes said. One of the things I've always loved about the Rails community is how much they embrace test-driven design, test-driven development in their educational material. I would love to see more testing in educational material.

[15:49] I'm guilty of it myself, because you just want to get to the functionality. I would love to see more of it.

Kent: [15:55] Yeah, I've seen some courses in places where, like, they're teaching a feature of JavaScript or something and they're using test-driven development to do it. It's a pretty interesting idea.

[16:08] Cool. Just to wrap this up here. If you could give our viewers one thing to improve their testing skills, what would it be? What's our homework?

Scott: [16:17] For me, it's just write more tests. That's true for a lot of things. Write tests and see what's working and what's not working. If you're writing tests for a feature and that feature still breaks, then that tells you something about the tests that you're writing.

[16:32] Just write more tests, experiment with it and find what works for you.

Wes: [16:37] For me, I'd say, go find that scary spot in your code base that, every time you have to work on that piece of the app, you go, ugh. If there's too much in your head, you're a little bit afraid to change stuff because it's working and you don't want to break it, try to write some tests for that piece of your application.

[16:54] That will for sure convert you and really show you that...coming back to confidence, it'll give you that confidence that that previously scary piece of your application is now a very confident piece of your app.

Scott: [17:10] That's great advice, yeah.

Kent: [17:11] Yeah, both super pieces of advice. Made me think of one more thing before we wrap up with the last question and that is, when I come to this really scary part of the application, and it's code that I definitely want to change. Eventually I'm going to refactor this to be a beautiful thing that's easy to change, if I need to change it frequently.

[17:32] If I'm going to test this, I want to make sure that my tests don't break, as I'm refactoring this stuff.

[17:42] Can you give any tips or ideas on things that you can do to avoid writing tests that break when you refactor? Specifically, when you change the code so that it does the exact same thing as it did before, it just does it in a more efficient way or it's easier to maintain the code or something.

[18:01] When I refactor I don't want my tests to break. What are some tips that you can give people to write tests like that?

Wes: [18:10] Go ahead, Scott.

Scott: [18:11] I was going to say, for me, it's mostly just testing outcomes. If I have small functions in my code, let's say I'm creating a shopping cart situation where maybe one function is calculating the subtotal and one is maybe adding in a coupon or something like that, is having the ability to test smaller chunks of that functionality.

[18:37] I don't want to make sure the cart's necessarily working, although I do, I want to check the small stuff. I want to check to make sure the coupon is working correctly, I want them to check to make sure the subtotal's updating correctly. I want to make sure those things are in place, so these smaller details, more micro stuff, I think, is very sort of important to test.

Wes: [18:58] Yeah, you stole my thing, Scott. Thanks a lot. I was going to say, just get granular, because if your tests do too many things, like some people say if you have the word and in your description, then you're probably going to have a hard time.

[19:14] If you can test the smaller little bits as well as the outcome of the entire thing, then you'll know along the way that you're not breaking the little bits.

Kent: [19:25] Let's just wrap up with the who or what would you like to give a shout out to. Doesn't have to be about testing, it can be if you want. Or you can plug your own stuff or plug other, you could plug each other's stuff, whatever you want to do there.

Wes: [19:39] These are called sick picks in the Syntax world.

Kent: [19:45] Scott, we'll have you go first.

Scott: [19:46] Yeah, in that regard, I'm going to plug Wes Bos' courses, they're all excellent. Every one of his courses, including the new upcoming, is it Advanced React? Is that what it is?

Wes: [19:57] Advancedreact.com, yeah.

Scott: [19:59] Advancedreact.com. I'm totally going to plug that course. I'm excited for Wes. Wes does testing, he does GraphQL, he does React stuff, it's all excellent. I'm going to plug that, great work.

Wes: [20:09] Awesome. I'm also going to plug my own stuff. [laughs] [inaudible] . On that Advanced React, the reason why people don't do testing in all the tutorials is because it's so much extra work. The entire course is 11 hours and then the added testing bit is 5.5.

[20:27] It's over half of what the original course is, just adding added testing things. I'm really excited about actually being able to offer that in there, as well.

[20:36] Obviously, I mean, sick pick, Scott, of course, is at leveluptutorials.com/store. He releases some new hotness every single month, and they're very good.

Kent: [20:46] Dude, the amount of material that you push out, Scott, holy.

Scott: [20:52] Hey. My computer is in the shop, it's been in the shop. Now it's like a third of the month is over and I'm trying to figure out how the heck I'm going to get the series done before the end of the month right now.

Wes: [21:03] Just do a whiteboarding coding interview.

Scott: [21:05] Good idea, got to get a whiteboard.

Kent: [21:09] Different formats, there's nothing wrong with that.

[21:12] Cool. Thank you both so much for your time. I appreciate it. I think this is helpful for people. A couple of takeaways for me is like, just try it out. You are new at this, that's fine. Focus on the stuff that is mission critical and then you can fill in other places later. You don't have to test everything, test the stuff that really matters.

[21:39] Any other takeaways for you all?

Scott: [21:42] Listen to Syntax at syntax.fm.

Wes: [21:44] Oh, yeah.

Kent: [21:45] Absolutely. I love listening to Syntax. It's one of the few podcasts that I still listen to now.

Scott: [21:52] That's great.

Wes: [21:52] Awesome. We made the cut.

Kent: [21:55] Yeah. [laughs] Thanks everybody. We'll see you all later.

Scott: [22:01] Bye.

Wes: [22:01] Peace.
