00:21:52,960 --> 00:21:55,430
Awesome. We made the cut. 

689
00:21:55,810 --> 00:21:58,700
Yeah.  Thanks everybody. 

690
00:21:58,700 --> 00:22:00,470
We'll see you all later. 

691
00:22:01,330 --> 00:22:01,630
Bye. 

692
00:22:01,630 --> 00:22:01,930
Peace.

fields.title: Snapshots and Reason with Jared Forsyth
fields.summary: Jared is a mobile & web developer for Khan Academy, he’s working to bring a world-class education to everyone.

fields.title: Static Types with Jessica Kerr
fields.summary: Symmathecist, developer, speaker, mother, crazy nut. Atomist. Learning and growing.
fields.transcript:
[00:01] Kent C. Dodds: Hi everyone, this is Kent C. Dodds here, and I'm here with Jessica Kerr. We're going to be talking about testing, and specifically static code analysis and the role that plays in quality of your code base. First, I'd like to give Jessica an opportunity to introduce herself. Jessica, could you tell us about who you are, what you care about, and what you're all about in the software world?

Jessica Kerr: [00:27] Sure. Thank you, Kent. I'm happy to be here. I'm about a lot of things in software. I have spoken at conferences about a dozen different languages. I've worked in Ruby, Scala, Java, and Clojure. Now I'm working in TypeScript, which I was nervous about, because JavaScript, right?

[00:53] It turns out, I've learned a lot about both JavaScript and static typing having worked in both dynamic and static languages. Man, TypeScript is a great balance. I no longer hate JavaScript, because JavaScript is just a large superset of a great language.

Kent: [01:18] I can go with that. I personally have had not a whole lot of experience with other languages, other than JavaScript. I started with Java, did a tiny bit of C. In school, I even did machine code, and that was nuts. Cool.

[01:40] It seems like you were a little apprehensive to jump into the JavaScript ecosystem, but TypeScript's made it OK for you. Sorry, did you have something to comment about that?

Jessica: [01:51] No, you have a really good point there, that writing in JavaScript is very different from jumping into the JavaScript ecosystem. We don't program it in languages, we program in programming systems, which includes all of our tools, and our runtimes. I program in TypeScript for Node.

[02:12] Which, coming from the JVM, the JVM is a very mature, carefully thought out designed runtime. Node is something we desperately needed. Now we have it, and it is what it is, but it is a whole ecosystem, and that's much more intimidating than the language itself.

Kent: [02:37] Actually, that's interesting that you mention that. Ryan Dahl gave that talk a while back about his regrets in Node. For those who aren't aware, Ryan Dahl created Node.

Jessica: [02:51] Brendan Eich has done the same thing about JavaScript.

Kent: [02:54] Yup, yup. The trick with JavaScript is because it's in the browser, we can't really, like with the JVM, and Java you can make breaking changes and things like that. With JavaScript, oh, is that wrong?

Jessica: [03:09] [distortion] that Java never had. It is a stable runtime. it's like a JVM is more stable than your code. Under the JVM is hardware, which is also fairly stable. They only have to update the JVM to accommodate new hardware. Whereas the browsers, there's many of them, and they're all changing. You don't have that stable dependencies property in the browser.

Kent: [03:40] Yeah, it's a different environment, for sure. Ryan Dahl was talking about how he was working on this new project called Deno. I think it was mostly an experiment, and we'll see, but it was based on TypeScript. Have you given any thought to that?

Jessica: [03:59] Oh, cool. No, I haven't. I should check it out.

Kent: [04:02] It's kind of an interesting idea. The language was TypeScript and lots of the mistakes he says he's made with decisions in Node, he has fixed with Deno. What role do you think static typing or static code analysis has in our testing? How is it different from manual or automated testing? How is it the same?

Jessica: [04:29] I would say that all of these are testing in the sense that testing is about answering questions about our code. It's about what does it do, and what does it not do?

[04:42] Testing in particular, like the unit tests and the integration tests that we think of as our primary tests, they test what it does do in certain circumstances. Static typing and static code analysis is often about testing what it doesn't do. In a sense, that's a more powerful assertion.

[05:06] Most knowledge is negative knowledge, and if we can have negative knowledge about our code, of what it doesn't do, then you get into what functional programmers sometimes get on about reasoning about code. All of this boils down to how do we answer the question of not only does it work, but how do we know it works?

[05:26] If you've got those assertions of you can be certain what it doesn't do, sometimes you can combine those into how do we know it will always work, as long as it compiles? At least, it won't do these other things that we don't want it to do.

Kent: [05:42] That's very interesting. I hadn't considered that. Like in science, you can't prove that something's right, you can just prove that other things are wrong, right?

Jessica: [05:49] Right, right.

Kent: [05:51] It makes sense, or there's an interesting corollary there, where static types can prove what it doesn't do. Can you elaborate a little bit on that? Maybe give us an example of like, I'm typing a function, how does static typing allow me to prove what it does not do?

Jessica: [06:12] If your function returns a certain object, static typing can make certain you'll never return null. It can make sure you never return an object that doesn't have those properties, or something completely different.

[06:31] In some languages, like Haskell, you can use static typing to prove that it doesn't change the world. It doesn't access the database. It doesn't print to standard out, other than possibly logging. Depending on the language, you can prove various degrees of what it doesn't do.

[06:52] In TypeScript, in particular, I find it pretty optimal, because you can choose to be like, "Yeah, yeah, I might return null, I'm not going to guarantee against that." Or, you can choose to have the compiler make certain your function doesn't return null.

Kent: [07:08] OK, yeah. Then, oh sorry.

Jessica: [07:10] Then you can say, "Don't get a null pointer exception." Or, what do you call that in JavaScript? Undefined is not a function.

Kent: [07:22] Yeah, that's great. It sounds like maybe I should just spend all of my time focusing on types, and then I never need to write any tests. Where's the benefit or what's the...First of all, is that true? Then second of all, if that's not true, then where's the line between static typing as a form of confidence and automated testing as a form?

Jessica: [07:51] That's a good question, and they're each suited to different situations. You're always going to want some tests, to show what your code does do. Unless you're talking about provably correct code, which is a lot of work.

[08:05] The trick with static typing is it can prevent you from doing things that are wrong, but if you wanted to do that, then you have to prove to compiler that you're doing it right. That can be really hard. Another thing I like about TypeScript, as opposed to say, Scala, is that it's pretty easy to override the compiler. 

[08:28] You can cast things to any, you can just tell it, "No, no, no, no. Don't worry about this, I know what I'm doing." You can get past a lot of those situations where, in Scala, you can play the game of make it so that if it's wrong, it won't compile.

[08:48] If you do that, good luck getting it to compile, ever. It's hard. That's easier in TypeScript, you can do that same thing with TypeScript, to a large degree, of make it not compile, if it isn't correct. Then you can always get past it, you can always override it. That's especially nice when you're just wanting to find out whether something works.

[09:14] When you're exploring, types are great when you can define correct. Let's face it, most of the time, in our user-facing apps, we don't know correct until we try it.

Kent: [09:28] Yeah, I think that's very true, and we also don't know the future either.

Jessica: [09:34] Oh, yeah.

Kent: [09:36] You can't tell the future from the present. Lots of times, I see people trying to guess the future, and they wind up creating a system that can cover every use-case they could ever imagine.

Jessica: [09:49] Which means it does none of them well or clearly.

Kent: [09:53] Yeah, exactly. When we get to that future, it's inevitably different from what we expected, and now it's harder to move over. Optimize for deletability, optimize for change, and that kind of thing.

Jessica: [10:05] I think the question of how should we write software is overrated. What we need to ask is how do we change software?

Kent: [10:14] Oh, that's so good.

Jessica: [10:15] That should be the core question of any architecture and any decisions in our ecosystems is, how do we change it? Because if you can change it, then you don't preclude possible futures.

Kent: [10:28] Wow. OK, I'm just going to, "Overheard, Jessica Kerr." That's great. I love that idea. I feel like static types can actually really help with that goal, because refactoring when you have static typing is just a world of difference.

Jessica: [10:51] In general, static types have the advantage of being readable by a computer. Tests, especially example tests, are useful to communicate to a human what should happen, and checking that that still does happen, which is very important for the question of not just how do you make a change in your software.

[11:11] How do you keep that change true in the face of change that's coming in from other people in the outside world? Static typing is readable by the computer, so we can have the computer enforce things. Which is what the compiler is doing. Then you can go farther with that.

[11:28] What I love about TypeScript, probably my favorite thing about it, is that you can decide how much strictness do you want in the compiler, and you can still add strictness later in the lint phase, because TSLint uses the TypeScript compiler to parse. You can do things like interrogate the types. Does this function return a promise?

[11:54] Oh, then I need to enforce that they awaited the result of that promise or returned it and didn't just instantiate it and let it go about its way without catching exceptions.

Kent: [12:09] That's great. I actually wanted to touch on linting versus typing a little bit. You mentioned that scenario. Can you tell us some other scenarios where, because static typing is so powerful by itself, what value does linting provide on top of static typing? You did give us an example, but can you touch on that a little bit more?

Jessica: [12:32] Sure, sure. In TypeScript, you have the option of moving checks from the compile phase to the lint phase. I think of, there's three clear testing, in the loose sense, check phases in TypeScript.

[12:51] There's compiling, there's linting, and there's testing. If you put something up front in the compiler, you get the advantage of red squigglies in your IDE. You can also get this from linting, but I turn it off because I can't frickin' stand the red squigglies about a stupid white space error that a computer is going to fix.

Kent: [13:09] Yup, I agree.

Jessica: [13:12] Wait, when I'm working on, OK, what really is correct, and I'm trying things out and seeing how they really fit together, I might have my tests running, but my code may not specify all its return types. I can have the compiler, I can set it to be strict, and say, "No implicit any," and you have to declare the types of everything.

[13:35] While I'm working in the code, I don't want to have to do all that stuff. I can move those checks out of the compile phase so that I can go ahead and run my tests. I can do whatever I want. I can push to the branch. Then the linter will fail if it can't automatically fix it.

[13:53] There's another thing that I love, love, about linting, is that you can just have a robot fix a lot of it. Much better than a naggy human. I don't want to nag anybody, and I don't really want to be nagged. The robot can fix it.

Kent: [14:07] Yeah.

Jessica: [14:07] We do a lot of that at Atomist, and support new ways of implementing that. You've got those three check options, and the compiler is for stuff that you want to give people hints about while coding. The tests are for checking that it's actively doing the thing we want it to do.

[14:27] The linting is for checking for a bunch of style things, but also some other features of JavaScript that you don't want to use. Linting is totally essential for JavaScript because it narrows the language to the subset that you actually want to use. During the experimentation phase, I don't care, do what I want.

[14:45] Once I need to share this code with other people, I do want to make sure I've declared all the types. I do want to make sure I've got the comments, so you can delay those checks, so that they don't get in your way while you're experimenting, while you're playing with it to figure out what it should really look like, but still enforce them before that code goes out into the wider world.

Kent: [15:09] I think that's great. Would you say that talking about the difference between the static code analysis, and the automated unit test integration, I find that difference to be close to static types can give me some pretty basic, well, pretty advanced idea of what the different types in my program are. 

[15:37] Then the automated testing piece is to check the business logic, because I can't really do that with a static type system. Is that where the line is for you, too?

Jessica: [15:53] I don't know. Personally, I tend to think in types in the sense that I tend to start my development with types. For me, I'm using those to express what goes into and what comes out of a function. I will do that before I write the interior of the function, but not everybody does that.

[16:16] Tests generally are for making those positive assertions of in this one circumstance, it does that. We haven't mentioned property tests yet. Those are runtime tests that -- runtime as in they run when you run the tests, not when you compile the code -- they can make assertions about what doesn't happen, as well as what happens in all cases, for some approximation of all.

[16:44] When they generate a bunch of random input, so you can be really specific about what is valid input, a lot more specific than just the types can be. You can be like, "This string should always be one word." You can't always do that in the type system.

[17:01] You can do more in a TypeScript type system than in say, Scala, in some senses, because literals are types, so you can specify exactly these strings. Like, my log function must receive a string that is either error, warning or info, and I can get a compile error, if I try to pass in a different string.

Kent: [17:27] Right. Is that enums, right?

Jessica: [17:30] No, that's literal types.

Kent: [17:32] Interesting.

Jessica: [17:32] You can also do it with enums, but you can just take string literals. OK, this is interesting. This comes out of TypeScript has the fascinating property of it's built on top of a language that has a ton of libraries, and it has to handle everything that people do in JavaScript. The compiler has to be a lot more flexible, or at least have the option to be.

[17:56] Then on top of that, they layer various restrictions that you can put into the compiler, that you can put into the linter. By default, it handles a lot of different things, and it's forced to handle some things that other languages never try to handle. jQuery is everywhere in JavaScript, or at least it was very everywhere and it's still around. We have to be able to type jQuery.

[18:21] jQuery is basically one function that, based on the first argument, which is a string, does totally different things. If we want to make types, we've got to know what that first string is.

[18:32] Therefore, TypeScript can treat a string literal as a type. If you call jQuery with element, the required parameters and the return value are something different than if you call it with the string something else class, I don't know.

[18:54] It's been a long time since I used jQuery. The point is that the fact that this existed in the ecosystem led to TypeScript implementing something that other languages never bothered with. That turns out to be really powerful because I love saying error-or, there's a pipe symbol for or, info-or, warning.

[19:16] I love being able to create my objects, and maybe I've got what I might have had to use inheritance for before. I can have data, just data objects, which if the kind is...Ah, shoot, this is going to be tricky. Say it's an address, if the kind is residential, maybe I don't require an attention to field, but if the kind is commercial maybe I do.

[19:52] You can distinguish between those based on string literals. Then you can do Boolean literals. True is a type, false is a type, numbers are a type. Because they're layering on top of this JavaScript ecosystem, they had to do things that introduce new and interesting things into the type system. I find it more expressive, even than Scala.

Kent: [20:21] I think it's really great, too, because static types in general, eliminate a whole category of bugs. Having such a powerful type system just makes that more pronounced.

Jessica: [20:36] Yes, and I love that it's entirely opt-in. JavaScript change the name to .ts, poof, you have typescript, as long as you have all the strict checking turned off. Then you can gradually add checks. The other thing that I love about linting is that you can always opt out.

[20:55] You can opt out by file, you can opt out by line, or by the rest of the file. You can opt out of linting in general, you can opt out of specific rules. That's really important. Sometimes I just need a line that's 120 characters. Sometimes, no, I really know I need to cast that as any and back, but I still want that check to make that discouraged.

[21:16] Then I can make my robot ping me in Slack, "Hey, do you want to opt out, or do you actually want to fix this?" I can push a button, and it adds the opt-out line.

Kent: [21:29] Wow, you really have some automation in there. That's nice.

Jessica: [21:33] That's what we do it at Atomist, is development automation, so I get to play with that stuff.

Kent: [21:40] Wow, that's very cool. I'm enthused by your enthusiasm around this. I hope everybody else is as well. I had one other question actually that's related to how you've been talking about the computer can format your code and things.

[21:58] I was wondering are you using Prettier, or is there some other automation? You're using the fix flags from TSLint, or how are you doing that?

Jessica: [22:07] We use TSLint auto-fix, and then also sometimes I make my custom fixes in Atomist, for if TSLint doesn't have an auto-fix, or if we want ours specially. One thing on my list is I need to make one. The TSLint parameter alignment thing, it's just not the way we want it.

[22:28] Conveniently, at Atomist, we have this auto-fix mechanism that will let us apply our little functions to the code. I do use TSLint auto-fix, and we have that one automatically applied. Instead of bugging us, it just pushes the commit to the repo.

Kent: [22:48] That sounds so great. Very cool. You should give Prettier a look, though. I think you'd be surprised how fun it is, and powerful it is in formatting your code.

Jessica: [23:01] Is it more flexible?

Kent: [23:03] Yeah, I think the biggest distinguish...

Jessica: [23:05] I think we had trouble with it. It did some things that we were like, "Not OK." 

Kent: [23:09] Oh, I see.

Jessica: [23:11] Maybe we just need to learn it better. 

Kent: [23:14] There is a little bit of flexibility there, but it's fairly opinionated. There are a couple of configuration options. If you want to jump into the Prettier camp, you get a lot of benefits, but you do have to just forget what you care about as far as code formatting, and just accept it.

Jessica: [23:33] Personally, I don't care, as long as it's consistent. I really don't care, let someone else worry about that. Other people have opinions.

Kent: [23:41] Yes, yeah.

Jessica: [23:41] My opinion is if you want to have opinions, write a program to make it so, and I'll happily let you run it on my code.

Kent: [23:48] That sounds like a pretty solid policy there. We're about ready to wrap up, I wanted to ask you if you could give everybody who's watching one item of homework, about static typing or static code analysis in general, what would that homework be?

Jessica: [24:13] That homework is going to be different for every person, because what I think everyone should, if you've got an hour or two, the most productive way you can spend that, for this little subset, look at the code formatting, typing, or linting tool that's already in your toolchain, and go read about it.

[24:37] Find what options, what configuration options it has. Become a power user of the tool you are already using. That's incredibly high leverage.

Kent: [24:49] That's fantastic advice. I feel like there's another piece of homework that you'd like to give, am I reading you wrong?

Jessica: [24:58] No, that was the one I was really excited about, because we can't learn everything. I really like conference talks, and conference videos played at 1.5 speed, for getting breadth of what's out there, and what could we do. Sometimes what's stopping us from doing something is we didn't know it was possible.

[25:27] For finding out what is possible, I like talks and videos, and Twitter. For going deep, if you can go deep on the tools that are already in your organization's chain, that's going to be really powerful. It makes you a wizard for the other people who are like, "Why is it doing this?" If nothing else, learn Git and your teammates will love you.

Kent: [26:01] That's for sure. I remember when I was getting started, the people who knew Git were invaluable to me.

Jessica: [26:11] Yeah.

Kent: [26:11] Thank you very much. Just as the last question, is there anybody or anything that you would like to give a particular shout-out to? Doesn't have to be related to testing, just anything that you'd like to either self-promote or whatever you'd like to let everybody know about.

Jessica: [26:27] I already talked a little bit about Atomist. That's the company I work at now. It's so fun to work at a company with complete alignment in objectives. Atomist is about letting us automate more of our own work.

[26:40] It's kind of like if you start with your continuous integration, your automatic builds and tests, and just zoom out and say, "If we get our heads out of this pipeline metaphor, what can we think about when we broaden software delivery, for instance, from individual repositories to the organization as a whole?"

[27:03] I think of Atomist to start with as a way to build your ideal software delivery tool. Many companies have their own stripe, built their own many years ago. Netflix built Spinnaker, and then published it, and that's a great deployment system for Netflix. Really, we each have our own environments that we're pushing to.

[27:25] We each have our own legacy software that we need to deal with, and we want to be able to migrate it, but to be able to migrate it, we have to take control of how we do that now, instead of scattering that across a bunch of Jenkins configurations, or YAML files, or Bash scripts. Really, why are we putting production code in Bash?

[27:45] Yes, your deployment is production code. That is essential to your business. Shout-out to Atomist, because it gives every company the ability to build the delivery system that they need, centrally. Also, various other kinds of development automation, like these automatic auto-fixes.

Kent: [28:08] Very cool.

Jessica: [28:09] Put the checks where you need the checks. Do things like run lint and test in parallel instead of one after the other. At my branch, I don't care if lint fails, but I want to know if the test fails, and I want to know if the lint fails, but I just don't want you to stop that from creating the artifact...OK, I could talk about that all day.

Kent: [28:25] It's been awesome chatting with you Jessica. Thank you so much for giving us your time. I think that people will benefit from these conversations, so I really appreciate it.

Jessica: [28:34] Thank you, Kent.

Kent: [28:35] Alright. Bye, everybody.

Jessica: [28:37] Bye.

fields.title: Testing culture with Justin Searls
fields.summary: Occasional conference talk speedrunner. Salt mining instructor at @testdouble.
fields.transcript:
Justin Searls: [00:00] Cheers.

Kent: [00:01] Hey everybody. It's Kent again here for this interview about testing, and I'm here with my friend Justin Searls. Say hi, Justin.

Justin: [00:10] Hey everybody.

Kent: [00:12] Justin is an awesome person. I'm going to go ahead and let him introduce himself. Justin, can you tell us a little bit about who you are, what you're all about in software, where you work -- whatever you want to say about yourself so that we can get some...

Justin: [00:26] I'm pleased to meet everyone. I was just learning a little bit about testingjavascript.com and the journey that all of the people, the customers who are working through all of these exercises are going through, and I just think it's fantastic.

[00:39] I've actually wanted to be building something like this for a long time, so learning that it exists is really exciting to me, and I know that it's in capable hands with Kent.

[00:48] A little bit about myself. My name's Justin Searls. I've been focused on helping people apply the same level of craft to their front-end code as their back-end code, which nowadays, with so many developers showing up and learning JavaScript as their first language, might sound a little bit silly.

[01:08] 10, 11 years ago, I was working on Agile, Extreme Programming team, and they would do test-driven development for 100 percent of their Java code or in their C# code. Then when it came time to build a complex user interface in JavaScript, it would be a Wild West. We would write one gigantic file of 100-line-long functions.

[01:29] There'd be no testing, and I'd show up to this. I'd be like, "Oh, really? Why don't we have this?" The answer was usually that the tooling wasn't there and there was no one in the organization to measure our coverage or hold us accountable.

[01:43] I said, "You know, honestly, I'd rather have a pretty good coverage on the front and the back side so that we're balanced than have 100 percent code coverage in the back end and then just a complete Wild West in the front end."

[01:57] I started at that point helping to build tools to make it easier to do automated testing in JavaScript on the front end. I spent a lot of time collaborating with our friends at Pivotal Labs on improving Jasmine.

[02:12] This would be 2009, 2010, 2011, in that era and building tools to make it as easy and painless and low friction as possible both for developers to run tests locally, get fast feedback, learn about their JavaScript and how it's working, and to arrive at better designs, but also to get them integrated into build systems and CI pipelines. A lot of that stuff is well-solved now.

[02:44] My primary interaction with the JavaScript testing community is through helping them take advantage of mock objects.

[02:53] At my company, Test Double, we maintain an eponymous library called Test Double, which is a mocking library. It's an alternative to something like SignOn or Jest's built-in mocking facility. It comes paired with a lot of really specific goodies to make sure that you're getting the most out of your relationship with mock objects.

[03:14] That's a little bit what I do. My day job, of course, is that I'm a cofounder and director of sales and marketing for Test Double, which is a consultancy of 50 developers around the country. We just work with teams and integrate with them getting stuff done, helping them improve. That's my very long introduction. That's why I'm here, who I am, what I do.

Kent: [03:35] You mentioned that you got to this company and everybody was doing great at testing their back-end code, but not the front end. Why did you care that the front end wasn't being tested? More clearly, what I'm looking for is why do you care about testing so much?

Justin: [03:54] I think that the thing for me, the reason that I care so much about testing was because my default settings are very high-anxiety and high fear that I'm on the wrong path or that I'm too dumb to figure something out, or that when I'm done, somebody else is going to find and be able to poke a whole bunch of holes in the work that I just did.

[04:20] There is a tremendous opacity with software, where no one really knows in an objective fashion what a good software application is or looks like. We can measure very specific, finite things, like does this transform A data into B data, or is this algorithm operating at a particular performance, like big O notation?

[04:41] When you're just at the end of the day say, "This is good code or bad code," we don't have a great language for that. Instead we fall back on a lot of chest-puffing and different displays that people do to articulate, "Hey, this is really great for xyz reasons." It becomes like a marketing exercise.

[05:02] I can play that game. I can be pretty good at that game, even being really good at selling to other people that your code is fabulous, it doesn't solve the fundamental problem, which is day in, day out, I want to feel tangible, incremental progress towards a goal and I want to have confidence that I'm on the right path, that I'm going to be able to shift something that works, that's valuable.

[05:24] For me, testing was an answer to that question of I'm staring at a blank screen. I need to know what to do next. Test-driven development specifically, but good test harnesses more generally, have given me an opportunity to know, in a paint-by-numbers fashion, this is the next thing that I need to do.

[05:44] I'm going to specify. I'm going to write a little bit of code that just exercises code that doesn't exist to make me go and write that code. It's going to be much more focused and directed.

[05:53] At the end of the day, if all I can say is it does these 25 things, then at least I know clearly it does these 25 things. I can be confident of that. That's, I think, the gestalt of what I find useful about testing.

Kent: [06:09] I think that's great. One of the things I love about test-driven development particularly is that it helps you stay focused. When you have a failing test, you know exactly what to do.

[06:22] The only trick is to motivate yourself and get yourself to write that failing test in the first place, which is normally...It's a learned skill. I'm not going to say it's easy. You get in the flow of it. It really helps you stay focused. You get distracted by Twitter or coworkers less easily.

[06:46] How would you say that testing influences the design of your code or how your code is architected? Especially if you're doing your tests first, it will probably influence the implementation that you come up with, I would expect.

Justin: [07:07] There's a couple big ones. I talk about mocking a lot and my very particular strategy for that. That requires lots of illustration to make any kind of sense. There's a couple talks that we can recommend later, that might do a good job of that.

[07:20] There's a couple really concrete things that are almost immediate benefits of trying any kind of test-first strategy, but especially test-driven development. That is that the design of the code is demanded by the existence of a test.

[07:40] If you're doing test-driven development, that means you're not writing a single line of new production code or functionality or feature, no new if branches for a particular option or something like that. You don't sneak something in like a guard clause at the top, just saying, "Hey, you know, if yadda-yadda is null, return." That stuff can only get added if you first have a test for it.

[08:02] It gives you high confidence that the design, through public interfaces, whether you're talking about unit tests that are calling a function or an integration test that's touching the user interface to trigger some behavior.

[08:15] In any case, every line of code there is provably exercisable and exercised by some test. If you're doing a really good job, that test will even be organized in a way to express the intention of the person who wrote it.

[08:31] Instead of just having a guard clause that says, "If this mysterious thing, then return," you'll have, hopefully, a test that's saying, "Hey, when the system is being configured in such and such a way, this is why we want this particular behavior."

[08:41] Which is where if you've ever heard of BDD, or behavior-driven development, that's where a lot of that came along, was to try to get better about using our tests to prompt us what we want.

[08:52] The final thing that we get out of any kind of test-first strategy that I think is really helpful is lots of reps, lots of practice. If you write a function and you're not writing any tests necessarily or you write tests after the fact, if you write that function, odds are, like 80 percent of the time, it's going to get called by only one other place in your entire system.

[09:16] Most of the functions that we write are only ever called in one place. Most of the objects, or maybe you're doing a lot of classes, they're only ever really used in one or two places.

[09:27] What that means is that we start to gravitate towards whatever API style we're familiar with or we feel like, or we're aping something that we saw somewhere else in terms of maybe a way to construct new objects or a particular pattern around multiple steps.

[09:45] Maybe we're always using promises and asynch away it even for stuff that doesn't necessarily need to be asynchronous. We can just fall into habit and incidentally, accidentally create overly complicated contracts between whatever we're writing and whoever needs to depend on that thing.

[10:05] When you're doing test-driven, if the thing under test needs to be constructed, it's going to happen like 18 times for the 18 different test cases in that test. If it's painful, that test is telling you, "Hey, maybe it's too hard to construct or to set up the environment for this thing."

[10:21] If you've got a walky-, multi-step API, those five or six lines when you're trying to invoke the behavior that you want, that's going to get really painful with every single test example.

[10:33] There's the design as it is, which is like, you will iron out a lot of that stuff while you're building your application and that's really useful. Then there's the design as it changes you, which is now your predilection is going to be for real simple API contracts between these things, and they have a particular shape.

[10:51] People who do TDD a lot tend to arrive at very similar-looking design patterns. The most common thing between them is they get really good at naming stuff and they get really good at making small things that do what they say on the tin. What is software, except we're trying to get good at that? Testing is kind of a backdoor way to get better at organizing concepts in our code.

Kent: [11:17] That all was fantastic, Justin. I've seen a lot of people have trouble doing test-driven development, even though they want to, trouble doing that particularly with UI code, because maybe I don't know what it is going to look like. Maybe I literally don't know what it's going to actually look like, because I don't have a design to implement.

[11:49] Maybe UI is just a category of its own that it's not good for test-driven or test-first methodologies. Do you have any advice or ideas about test-first methodologies around UI?

Justin: [12:03] Yes. There is a lot to say and I'll try to keep it brief. I know I'm a blabbermouth. The biggest thing that I would want to impart to anybody who's getting serious about testing is to get good at a methodology, you have to commit completely.

[13:16] For example, sometimes I meet people who are trying to do test-first with an outside-in approach. Maybe they're fighting with Selenium or whatever browser automation tool that they're using and that's causing pain.

[13:30] Maybe they have a team and that team is changing text and classes and markup underneath them and there's naturally going to be more moving parts, so there's lots of incidental changes happening. That's the increased cost of change and of risk.

[14:59] One of the things that I think was one of the best ideas out of the behavior-driven development community, folks like Zach Dennis, Dave Chilemsky, Matt Wynn and Aslak Hellesøy between RSpec and Cucumber...They kind of championed this seven, eight years ago.

[15:17] What they said was, "Start a new feature." The first step of that feature, once you have basic requirements, is write that fully integrated test that's called an acceptance test, usually. [15:49] Once you get to that good failure test, the logical failure, then you step in, so to speak, into that smaller concentric ring of lots and lots of iteration of lots of small units to alternately make the big thing go green.

[16:47] If you're able to do this well, it can supercharge your team's focus, direction and productivity in a way that just picking features off in an indefinite, infinite backlog and viewing everything as an endless grind can slow teams way, way down because it's not quite so motivating.

Justin: [45:41] You can watch it in half speed if you need to, or work through it a couple times. I try to be as dense as possible, because I want to really value your time. I put a lot of love and effort into those talks. I hope that they have an impact.

Kent: [45:56] It was fantastic. Hey, thank you so much, Justin. I'm sure that people really appreciated this, or they will in the future, when they watch this. [laughs]

Justin: [46:05] Right on.

Kent: [46:07] Thank you, and we'll see everybody later. Bye.

Justin: [46:11] Bye-bye.

fields.title: Testing Levels with Mattias Johansson
fields.summary: Mattias is a Software-assisted mental health treatment at http://Mindler.se and the creator of the programming show Fun Fun Function on youtube.
fields.transcript:
Kent Dodds: [00:00] Hey, everybody. It's Kent C. Dodds here with my friend, Mattias. You probably know him as mpj...Wait, what is your Twitter?

Mattias Johansson: [00:11] The Twitter is mpjme, which is the same as my website.

[00:16] [crosstalk]

Kent: [00:17] It's Mattias Johansson, right?

Mattias: [00:19] Yeah, exactly. The reason for mpj is that Mattias Johansson is an extremely common name in Sweden. It's basically like John Smith.

[00:30] I added a letter in the middle because that makes it slightly less common, but then nobody knows how to pronounce it, so it's just mpj.

Kent: [00:38] Yeah, I'm particular about the "C" in my middle name, partially for [laughs] SEO purposes, and it's easier to get Kent C. Dodds. Although I haven't ever run into another Kent Dodds on the Internet before, so it's worked out OK for me. [laughs]

[00:53] Why don't we get an intro to you for the people who may not have been introduced to you yet.

Mattias: [01:02] I'm mpj. I'm mostly known because I run a YouTube channel called Fun Fun Function, which is sort of a cooking show for programmers.

[01:11] It started out as tutorials. Nowadays it's mostly a show that you can tune into every week that is supposed to give you this feeling of, "Hey, those people on the camera, they seem to be having fun. It seems like they are excited about creating things." Perhaps I lost a little bit of the joy in creation and I'm trying to find that again. The show helps you do that.

[01:38] The show is also about showing real people on screen. In the programming world, people tend to only showcase their successes. When you do a tutorial, everything is like, ooh, super smooth. We show the mistakes and we talk about the mistakes in our career, as well.

[01:56] It also leaves you with a sense of, "Hey, I can do that. It's about, hey, that seems fun, and hey, I can probably do that as well."

Kent: [02:06] That's great. I livestreamed creating my component downshift. That's the autocomplete component for React.

Mattias: [02:15] Nice.

Kent: [02:16] People have told me they really appreciated that because they watched me go through all of the struggles of, oh wait, what's that API called again, and having to Google around for a bunch of things. I think that's great that you do that.

[02:29] People need to be reminded that the face that we present to the world, or that most people present to the world is the perfection without any imperfection, but that's only part of the story.

Mattias: [02:46] Yeah, true. I notice I kind of view as part of my job to appear vulnerable onscreen, because almost nobody dares to do that.

Kent: [03:01] Let's get some vulnerability here now. [laughs]

Mattias: [03:04] Let's. I like that.

Kent: [03:06] We're talking about testing. First off, anybody who's followed you for a little while knows that you care deeply about testing. Can you talk about why it is that you care so much about testing?

Mattias: [03:21] Lots of reasons. The big reason is that when I discovered testing, it made me a much more focused programmer. I'm kind of attention-deficient. I have a hard time focusing on a task and I get very, very easily distracted.

[03:43] Unit testing allows you and forces you to break your work into these very nice little atoms of units of work. It gives you "OK, now the test is red. Now I write the code to make it green and now I can commit."

[03:59] It becomes an extremely, extremely nice and focused way of working. That is actually what I like most about it.

[04:08] It also makes me calmer. I'm also a very worried person. My brain tends to find little things in my daily life and in programming too that it can just spin into complete catastrophes. Unit testing also gives me a sense of security, like "OK, that is now tested. Now I have tests running for that."

[04:32] I would say focus and a sense of security.

Kent: [04:38] That makes me think of how I am when I'm pair programming. I can't get distracted by Twitter when I'm pair programming or when I'm livestreaming as well.

[04:50] It's cool to think of the idea that test-driven development or having some methodical approach toward testing -- goal-oriented approach, I guess -- can really help you remain focused as you're developing software.

[05:08] I can totally relate to being worried. I remember a while back I had a coworker who needed me to update something with one of my libraries and I went through, I added a test, I implemented the fix. Then I just pushed it and it published. He was just like, "That's all you're going to do?" I said, "Yeah, I've got really good testing coverage on here. I feel pretty confident that this is going to be fine."

[05:36] I remember he was so, "Oh, man. I never would feel comfortable doing that." Testing can give you that assurance and that confidence. It's not perfect, but automated testing will do a lot more checking for me than I would do if I were doing it all manually.

Mattias: [05:59] Oh, my God, I also hate repetition. I think I hate manual testing way more than other programmers, so I just don't do it really. I had this person [inaudible] to introduce to testing. He was an old-school developer and it was many years ago. He was like, "No, I don't need it because I test my code."

[06:23] There are problems with that [laughs] argument, even if it does. In the case of me, I have a really hard time performing manual smoke tests. It just irks my soul somehow. I'm willing to do a lot of work to make it automated.

Kent: [06:45] Even when you're developing the software -- let's talk about UI specifically -- what's your workflow for developing the software? Do you even have the browser up to see what things look like? Or, is it pretty exclusive in the terminal, or whatever testing framework you're running, doing test-driven development?

Mattias: [07:06] Right now, to be honest, I don't do much development. I'm in a kind of situation where making YouTube videos takes up most of my time. It's actually a very small segment of development. Most of it is preparing the code I'm going to write, and then I'm editing, then [inaudible] .

[07:29] Frameworks like Enzyme that actually made unit-testing UI sort of bearable only appeared in the very last parts of the time when I was a full-time developer at Spotify. I didn't really develop some good flow for that. When it came to the absolutely upper layer of the UI, I just didn't test it because the tooling was just not good enough.

[08:06] The small project I've done with Enzyme...I'm a big believer of doing prototyping and then throwing things away. Whenever I'm working with a new API, when I try to figure out how I want an interface to look like with HTML, I tend to just write it in the dirtiest way possible, almost intentionally dirty so that I could not possibly be tempted to use this piece of shit code, just to figure out where I want to go.

[08:44] I just copy/paste it to a file or something, then I just revert to repository, and then I start doing it test-driven. That's the way I found working with API and shallow [inaudible] DOM testing. Otherwise, it's like trying to simulate it in your head. That's hard for me. I actually try to have my brain run as little code as possible and try to rely on the computer to do that.

Kent: [09:16] I think that UI has historically been really challenging to test drive. The tools encouraged testing implementation details. When you're testing implementation details, you cannot test drive that very well because you don't know how you're going to implement it. You just know what kind of interface it should have.

[09:43] Next time you get into testing UI, you should check out react-testing-library if you're doing React. That explicitly disallows or makes it hard to test implementation details. Test-driven development, at least for me, I always struggled with test-driving with Enzyme. When I came up with react-testing-library, I tried it again, and I found it was actually pretty straightforward.

[10:12] In this course that I'm launching as part of this interview series, I have a couple of videos to show how to do test-driven development with React Testing Library. You can check that out when -- actually I'll give you early access. You got this.

Mattias: [10:28] Thanks. What we could do is I'll have you on the show, Fun Fun Function, and you teach me how to do it.

Kent: [10:40] Yeah, that would be cool. Let's do it. Maybe I'll add a link to that video in the notes of this video.

Mattias: [10:49] Yeah, depending on my scheduling, we will promote somehow.

Kent: [10:55] Cool. I wanted to ask you, what do you think is something that people get wrong with testing? Maybe when we're starting testing at a company, we've never really done it before. We've been doing it for a long time, and we get into this rut of a way that we're always doing it and we're doing it suboptimally.

[11:16] What are some things that you see people are getting wrong with testing?

Mattias: [11:21] I'm not sure exactly what people get wrong with testing. If people just do a lot of testing, you get better at it. I think the biggest problem is more like people don't do testing.

[11:37] I think it's rare in an organization to see that the organization does testing fundamentally wrong for years. I'm more interested in what organizations do that creates hurdles that makes it hard to test.

[11:58] One of the big things that people often don't get is that if you write your code without tests that makes the code very hard to test. You don't modularize your code -- it's still a very hard word for non-native speaker.

Kent: [12:20] It's a hard word for a native English speaker, too. [laughs]

Mattias: [12:27] You don't modularize then your code is not in modules. They're not in units, so you can't unit test them. You need to actually start adding tests just to make your code testable.

[12:48] Also, people have a hard time getting tests introduced because they don't know what code to test. It just becomes extremely difficult. OK, are we going to refactor this big ass module now just to add the first test? The first tests becomes an extremely big hurdles.

[13:15] My best approach there that I found worked [inaudible] is that every new commit should have unit tests with it, or a good excuse about why it was impossible. That tends to over time drive a testing culture I found, it becomes reasonable.

[13:42] That gives you a sort of an autonomy and an out to avoid the situation where this absolutely satanic pile of shit that is going to take ages to break apart to modules.

Kent: [14:00] Going with the policy of increasing coverage over time, it's actually similar to if you wanted to rewrite an app from Backbone to React or something, which I've done before, or Backbone to AngularJS, which I did longer ago.

[14:18] If you try to rewrite the whole thing [laughs] in one commit, it's not going to happen.

Mattias: [14:22] No, it's not.

Kent: [14:24] Iteratively, new features are written in this new framework, new features are written with tests. Occasionally, somebody will say, "Hey, I'm going to take a day or two and I'm just going to add a whole bunch of tests for this area of the app, just because I finished with my sprint work or whatever."

[14:42] There's nothing wrong with that. Trying to have this monstrous commit that changes things to make it modular and testable, that just will fail in sad ways.

[14:57] You talked a little bit about what do you test and what do you not test. Can you expound on that a little bit? How do people develop an intuition of what should be tested and what should not?

Mattias: [15:16] What I recommend, if you're not sure about what to test and not what to test, then yes, run with the practice of test every goddamn thing. Yes, bang your head onto things until you feel like, OK, I'm very comfortable with writing tests now.

[15:36] Now I know that the reason why I'm having a hard time writing this test is because this is a fundamentally complicated and [inaudible] thing to test. That allows you to practice the craft of testing and eventually develop a taste in where it's worth it and where it's not.

[15:58] That's the only way of doing it. Writing unit tests is almost as broad as coding itself. It's just like this inverse coding thing. There's no real simple rule. I've developed a rule for when it's appropriate to do tests first though and when it's not.

[16:24] I've found that whenever you're working with a known API, test first is nice. For instance, if you have a service that you wrote, that you're very familiar with, that you've written to be testable, and have written more code that interacts with this piece of code, then it makes sense to test drive it. You are able to very easily simulate the effects in your head.

[16:55] However, if you're working with an external API, for instance, a new credit card API, then it's going to be extremely hard for you to test drive that. You're not really sure exactly what the outputs are. The outputs of your ordering system is going to be commands to this credit card system.

[17:18] You're going to test, is this now generating the correct command when we are putting an order into the shopping cart and into the checkout? Given that this payment request command returns this response, this happens.

[17:37] Since you're not familiar with this thing, it's just not really going to work. You need to actually have a phase where you're prototyping against it and just coding not test-driven and more like fiddle with the API-driven, so that you figure out what exactly the calls are going to look like.

[18:00] In that case, I also use fallback to that approach that I talked about before where you just do this throwaway prototype in order to research and navigate and figure out inputs and outputs. Then and only then, you start test driving it and verifying that your code produces the end results that you want.

Kent: [18:33] You mentioned earlier too that if you're just getting into testing, you don't find that people will do the wrong thing for a long period of time. They'll figure it out eventually.

[18:45] Is there some tips or ideas that you can give people so that they can accelerate their process of getting to the point where they're happy with their test base? Because in that process of learning, you're going to do things that you're like, "Oh wow, that was a bad idea. Like, now every single time I even touch this code, I have to update the test. Like, that is not, that's not good."

[19:11] What are some tips that you could give to people to accelerate their experience in testing in a way that gives them the confidence without all of the drags?

Mattias: [19:26] I think that the most important thing is to try to extract as much joy from it as possible. I think that I had a bit of a luxury because I've grown up as a programmer in a situation where I was the one driving and pushing for unit tests.

[19:45] I think that a lot of big generation now programmers are getting unit tests shoved down their throats. That means that you're going to be on the defensive. Unit tests might become this chore for you. Try to step back from that. Start seeing unit tests as this thing that gives you a feedback cycle.

[20:08] You want it to feel like a computer game. A computer game is designed to give you these very small reward cycles of feedback. You get a task. You perform it. Then you get some kind of reward. That's the way that unit tests are supposed to feel like.

[20:25] It has to be fast. If you have a test Tweet that is slow, for instance, or doesn't allow you to run your test in a quick manner or that doesn't run automatically, then your brain is not going to get that dopamine kick that is so, so, so nice when you get unit-testing working again. That is what really gets you interested in it. That's what makes it powerful.

[20:55] Try to just work with that and feel like am I feeling joy when writing these things? If you're not, try to feel like can I stand back and improve the test suite or the test [inaudible] somewhat? Can I perhaps add some auto-test here? Why isn't this running quickly in the continuous integration suite? Or anything that just makes the flow quick, smooth, and painless.

Kent: [21:30] That reminds me of the Jest testing frameworks mantra, painless testing or something. At least that's been my experience. It's been pretty great.

[21:46] You reference unit test quite a bit. Can we talk a little bit about the distinction between unit and integration and end-to-end testing?

Kent: [21:53] Can we?

Mattias: [21:55] First, let me give my definition because I feel like one of the big challenges in our industry is that people will define these terms differently. Because I'm primarily a frontend engineer, I do plenty of Node stuff. Most of it is tooling. I don't do a lot of services. I pretty have never written a legit production service in Node or in any language for that matter or platform.

[22:23] When I'm talking about integration versus unit, I'm saying a unit would be like your button component or maybe a form input. Then if you're doing an integration test, that'd be like a form that has multiple fields and then there's a submit button. Where do you draw the line between these different forms of testing? Where do you feel like each makes the most sense?

Mattias: [22:46] A couple of times a year, I always fall into the debate with somebody who's just gotten into testing. They figure out that why do we need these unit tests? We can just replace this with end-to-end tests completely. Then we cover everything, which is a trick everybody does including myself. I have done exactly this.

[23:10] Then you realize that that theoretically works, but end-to-end tests are extremely expensive and brittle, which is why you want unit tests. You start seeing the economy component of it which you don't see when you're starting out because then you just see the mental abstract model.

[23:34] I have been in a couple of situations where we tried some integration tests. When I write apps where I'm allowed to do whatever I want to do, then I tend to just do unit tests and end-to-end tests. I build these apps where I can put the database into a known state with a set of orders.

[24:06] Then I have a little interface that a browser can go click in to set them in these states, like, "I am now this user. I am now this user. I am gonna impersonate this." Then it navigates, clicks through, makes an order, and make sure that that's OK.

[24:23] Then to complement that, I have a ton of unit tests. I don't actually do anything in between. It's just horizontal, horizontal, horizontal, horizontal. Then torpedo through a few end-to-end tests. I'm not saying that that necessarily is the be-all end-all. After many years of testing, this is the approach that I currently own.

[24:55] At Spotify, we tried this thing where we had a PhantomJS browser, headless thingy that spun up parts of the app, because the app was built out of these small iFrame components. It spun those up and clicked on them, but the backend requests...

[25:22] Because Spotify has this special thing you can think of as rest endpoints actually. What it did is that it simulated the rest endpoints, like that's where the mocking layer was and then the entire layer above that was the tested thing. I didn't really like that. I found those tests to be really icky to write. It was easy for simple scenarios.

[26:00] For example, when it came to infinite scroll lists, it became what REST requests the infinite scroll components produces was so complex that it was just...You captured it. Then you pasted it into the tests. You weren't really sure what the hell you were doing. It just eh. That's a type of integration test that I found that didn't work.

[26:40] Other than that, I don't think that I've had much experience with integration tests which are not system tests. Perhaps you can talk a little bit about that.

Kent: [26:51] It sounds like a lot of the struggle that you were having was related to the tools of the time. I feel like the tools that we have now are a little bit more powerful and flexible.

Mattias: [27:11] Wait, no. The [laughs] tooling was actually not the problem here. That's not what I was trying to get across actually.

[27:18] Problem was more like it was trying to test too many layers. There was the DOM layer. Then there was this little database layer that generated requests for data that was not in the cache. Then that generated these HTTP requests. Then we had this mocking at the bottom.

[27:45] The output was so complex because there were so many layers involved in the integration. Better tooling would not really have helped because it was not accidental complexity.

Kent: [28:02] When I'm doing UI integration tests, I'm mostly interested in the integration from one component to another. I'll still mock out all HTTP requests and things. Maybe that wouldn't help in your specific scenario where the response that you're mocking is supposed to be pretty complex.

[28:23] Most of the time, I find the unit tests are really great for the edge cases. One of the nice things about unit tests is that they run so fast. They're pretty easy to write. The integration tests get most of those benefits. They're really fast and pretty easy to write so long as you're mocking at the right layer. Then you get more coverage.

[28:50] My integration tests are generally more Happy Path stuff, more normal case. Sometimes, you only really can test an actual error case scenario in an integration test. Most of the time, I focus my edge case testing in unit tests. Then my end-to-end tests are mostly just a handful of Happy Path for most of the reasons that you mentioned. They're a lot slower, more finicky.

[29:20] Now that we have Cyprus...I'm actually wearing my Cyprus shirt.

Mattias: [29:23] [inaudible] to check that thing out.

Kent: [29:28] It's fantastic. Lots of the finickiness, the brittleness, and the difficult to write and slow aspects of end-to-end tests, that's slowly becoming less of a problem. It's definitely still way slower. The tooling isn't quite there.

Mattias: [29:47] It's always going to be because it's spinning up a lot of things. A lot of things can go wrong. Thus, a lot of things will go wrong.

Kent: [29:57] Exactly. This is the big trade-off as you go up the...I don't believe in the testing pyramid. I have this thing, the testing trophy. As you [laughs] go up the testing trophy or the testing pyramid, the opportunity for those tests to fail increase. Even if one small thing is broken, you can break many tests.

[30:23] Whereas with unit tests, that's less of a problem, if one small thing is broken, then the unit test for that thing will break. It's a lot easier to identify very quickly what the broken thing is.

Mattias: [30:34] Couldn't we go back to the thing where we talked about what level to mock? What to mock is the continuing mystery of every unit tester. It's the first thing you are confused with when you're starting out, and then you continue to be confused until you die.

[30:57] [laughter]

Mattias: [31:00] I haven't reached that point yet. If you go back to the Spotify application that I talked about, you have the upper UI, and then there's some business logic, and then that operates through...We had a kind of ORM layer, which also had a bunch of caching.

[31:24] If stuff was cached, it made requests to the HTTP service for the missing parts. It was these requests that was mocked. I felt like this was too many layers in one. Where would you have started mocking? Which parts would you have integrated?

[31:48] If you don't think that this is applicable, perhaps you can talk about an application where you talk about the integration pairs.

Kent: [31:59] I think this is a pretty good example. Often you're going to have a module, like a client side module, that's responsible for making those requests, and that's normally the module that I'll be mocking.

[32:10] Let's say that we're testing a React application. That's what I work in mostly. There will be a module. In your componentDidMount, you'll make a request, or componentDidUpdate, or whatever. I will mock the module that is responsible for making the requests.

[32:29] I feel like that's a pretty good layer of abstraction, for mocking purposes. Then in end-to-end tests, you don't mock anything. Just to make sure that, just save your bacon on, "Oh, looks like my mock was wrong."

[32:47] Which is the constant struggle of mocking, but for most things in integration tests, it's pretty much, most of the time, when I'm integration testing UI, the only thing I mock is the network requests, or potentially, if you rely on it, geolocation.

[33:12] Then, with unit tests, you're going to be mocking a lot more to isolate things. That's why generally, if I have a component that is used by a lot of other components, but it doesn't really have edge cases, I probably won't unit test that, because I know it's going to be covered in my integration tests.

Mattias: [33:30] That's an interesting point. The point where the unit test is the horizontal scale, so if you have things torpedoing through your app, you don't really need unit tests.

Kent: [33:42] Yeah, and sometimes I will, because sometimes those unit tests can, like I said, when you're integration testing, if one small thing breaks, you're going to have 30 tests that break, all at once. You're like, "What is going on?"

[33:58] That's a big criticism that I get, with my integration test-focused approach, is that people are like, "Well, when something breaks, everything breaks, and that's not fun." It's true, it's not very fun.

[34:11] The alternative view is if you say, "Let's just focus on unit tests," then you could have an integration point that's broken, and you can't cover that with unit tests. If you've got a really good suite of end-to-end tests, then maybe you're covered, but there is the trade-offs there.

[34:30] I'd rather have a handful of pretty good end-to-end tests, a bunch of integration tests that cover the happy path of most of my components, and then a handful of unit tests that are going to cover the edge cases for these components.

[34:44] That seems to have worked really well for the applications that I'm building.

Mattias: [34:51] Yeah. While you were talking, I came to think of a more disciplined mental emotional aspect of unit testing. When you said that, "Hey, we don't need unit tests for this because there's no edge cases."

[35:10] One interesting aspect of code is that even the worst module that was written by Satan himself, started out as very simple. A big mistake that I fall, like a thinking trap that I've fallen into so many times, is that, "Hey, this piece of code is so simple, it just has one execution path, it doesn't need any unit tests."

[35:35] Then I start writing it, and then I add this little extra thing, and then it just becomes a slippery slope into an extremely complex component. It's so hard to figure out a limit there. It's such a nasty gray area, that in order to not have to think about that, I tend to just write unit tests for everything, anyway.

Kent: [36:06] Yeah, that's a much simpler rule, and I'd be fine with that. The cost, especially for a simple component like that, the cost of adding a unit test, so long as it's a well written unit test that doesn't test implementation details, the cost is pretty low.

Mattias: [36:24] Yes.

Kent: [36:24] It's worth it to have a simple rule. I'm on board with that, for sure.

Mattias: [36:29] Since the cost is low, yeah, that's a good point.

Kent: [36:32] If you could give the people watching, one thing that they could do to improve testing, like give them some homework to do, what would that be?

Mattias: [36:43] Do a lot of it. I'm a big believer of just throwing time at a problem. I don't believe in much hacks. Try to observe the process as much as possible, and try to reason about how could it do this better? How can I make this easier? How can I help my colleagues write better tests?

Kent: [37:07] That's great. Yeah, I like that. There's no shortcut to experience.

Mattias: [37:15] Nope. Sorry.

Kent: [37:17] One last question before we wrap up, is there anyone or anything that you'd like to give a particular shout-out to? Doesn't have to do with testing, just anything in particular.

Mattias: [37:28] For me, the book that got me interested in testing, is "Test-Driven Development By Example." I really, really like that. I think it's written by Kent Beck. I still recommend it. It's just really good. It doesn't use JavaScript or anything, but it just makes you get the thing.

Kent: [37:54] That was written back in 2003, or something. Kent Beck is the rediscoverer or the creator of test-driven development. I think he knows a thing or two [laughs] about that. Cool, yeah, I'll make sure to have a link of that in the notes for our interview.

[38:13] Mattias, it's been a pleasure, it was awesome. This is actually the first time we've talked in person, so that was also very nice.

Kent: [38:22] Yeah, yeah. Cool, thank you very much, and we'll see everybody later.

Mattias: [38:27] Bye.
