fields.title: Testing Practices with J.B. Rainsberger
fields.summary: J.B. runs a consulting agency jbrains.ca. He writes books about software development, training, coaching and also speaks at conferences.
fields.transcript:
Kent C Dodds: [00:01] Hey everybody, it's Kent again. I'm here with JB Rainsberger. I didn't actually ask you how to say your last name. Is it Rainsberger?

JB Rainsberger: [00:08] [laughs] That was pretty good, yeah. Rainsberger.

Kent: [00:10] [laughs] I had a couple people recommend that I talk with JB. This is the first time we've met. JB, could you give us a little intro to yourself for those who may not know who are you? Maybe tell us a little bit about what you're all about in software and specifically in testing.

JB: [00:30] Absolutely. I guess people know me as one of the original JUnit guys. I became interested in extreme programming right around 2000. At that time, my biggest problem as a programmer was I kept drowning in my own bugs so I started to look for how do I fix this problem.

[00:54] I ended up finding JUnit. I ended up finding the original books on extreme programming. Now I tend to spend most of my time writing about and teaching tester and development around Europe, because nobody will hire me back in Canada.

[01:19] Over the years, I've mostly been known as a TDD teacher and one of these programmers who focuses on the classic XP idea of technical excellence. What I've done over the last 5 to 10 years is I sprinkle some other topics into that work.

[01:40] Usually by the end of a three-day course in TDD, it's 3:00 in the afternoon on the third day, people are tired, and I say, "Hey, OK. We've talked about a lot of good stuff. I showed you some techniques. We talked a lot about code. We talked a little bit about testing. Now tell me the 27 reasons why this isn't going to work."

[02:00] That's when we get to talk about things like the people problems involved. How do I organize the work? What are the trade-offs in writing tests or not writing tests? How do you do it, and that kind of thing? When I work with people, mostly it starts with how do we do TDD better? It ends up somewhere else entirely.

Kent: [02:20] That's very interesting. I think it's an important point, maybe not just in testing, but in anything in software is, here's the optimal. Here's how you do all these things. Now let's talk about it practically in a pragmatic situation where you have deadlines and you have different priorities and things.

[02:41] Maybe testing that one little utility function that's for your Easter egg in your app, that might not be worth the time. Let's focus our efforts on the parts of the app that are more important. Taking these philosophies and these ideas, and coming up with more pragmatic viewpoints around them, I think, is really wise.

JB: [03:03] I think a lot of people, they're very eager to make sure that they evaluate those trade-offs well. one of the things that people just need help getting comfortable with is the idea, the old saying that "Good judgment comes from experience, and experience comes from bad judgment."

[03:20] The best thing that I can do is to say, "On the one hand, here are all the things that I think about when I'm making these trade-off decisions. On the other hand, it's OK that you're going to get that wrong for some value of wrong for the next couple of years, so did I."

[03:35] There's lots of things that I do now that I wouldn't have done 15 years ago. There's lots of things that I know how to do now that I had no idea how to do 15 years ago. Good news, when I started doing test-first programming, I saw a huge improvement in my work even before I knew all the cool, advanced techniques that I use in my design now.

[04:00] I hope that that gives people a little bit of a liberating feeling. Just go start. Yeah, you're not going to be a superstar in the first week, neither was I, it takes time to get there. If bugs are your big problem, then you don't have to worry about squeezing every last bit out of the technique.

[04:18] Just start paying attention to what you're writing. Things will get a lot better. Then you'll have the energy to figure out and learn all the rest of the cool stuff. I'm happy to teach that too.

Kent: [04:27] That's great. I'm really glad that you mentioned that. I think a lot of people are overwhelmed with all of the things that you have to think about testing. There are a ton of terms. Testing, for some reason, also has just a huge, wide variety of opinions on how you should do it properly.

[04:48] For beginners, I think, it's just really overwhelming. I'm glad that you mentioned that it's OK. You can figure this out over time. Over time, you develop an intuition for what works and what doesn't. There's no shortcut to experience.

JB: [05:08] Remember that intuition comes from practice. Intuition is exactly that. It's I've done this so many times that I don't have to think about every little detail anymore. I can think at a higher level. Then you have that moment where you think, "Oh, I should do this" and you can't.

[05:25] If I stop you and force you to articulate what you were thinking, you could probably do it, but the idea is just so obvious in the moment. That is just, it's going through all the steps really quickly in your head like that. As you say, you don't get that by anything other than practice.

Kent: [05:44] JB, why don't we talk a little bit about why you care so much about testing? Why is testing important to you? Why can't we just write our application code? Why do we have to spend all this time writing code that our users never see?

JB: [05:59] It started for me with a simple, obvious personal problem. Like all good stories, it started with pain. Here I was, I am in IBM. I'm growing up as a programmer. I've worked for a couple of years professionally and I start to notice that I can't seem to write code without making mistakes.

[06:18] I don't mean perfectionism. I mean I fix the problem and create two more, fix a problem, create two more, fix a problem. "Ooh, I got that one." I'm 25 years old. I have two years of professional experience. It already feels like I'm drowning.

[06:35] I have this pain in my chest, this stress that keeps eating away at me. I promise I'm not being overly dramatic. I'm not exaggerating when I say, I had a moment where I said, "I'm 25 years old. I can feel the heart attack coming. I don't know how I'm going to do this for 40 more years.

[06:52] I did the only sensible thing. I opened up Netscape Navigator 3 and I went to AltaVista.com. I typed into the search bar "how to test Java code." I figured, "Well, why should I have to wait for a tester on another floor to tell me that my code is wrong? I know my code is wrong. I just don't know how my code is wrong. I've got to be able to get this information for myself."

[07:18] I didn't even realize at the time that I was living one of the extreme programming values of looking for feedback. That's what I did. How to test Java code, and that's how I found JUnit and that's how I found a draft of this weird book called "Extreme Programming Installed." I read that and I fell in love.

[07:37] Here is an approach to doing software development that focuses in part on getting feedback about reality and adjusting your plan to match reality instead of trying to bend reality to fit your plan. Most people at that time, when they encountered the ideas from Extreme Programming, either it was "No, this will never work" or "Love it, everyone should do it this way," and I was the second one.

[08:04] That's when I started to learn about the value of testing as a programmer, starting to understand the difference between the kinds of tests that a programmer needs for themselves and the kinds of tests they need to negotiate with their customer and the kind of tests they need, other more skilled testers to do.

[08:22] I was a big fan of Ron Jeffries' idea that if we do our job well, we put testers out of business, but that doesn't work. What we do is we free them up to do actual testing, this kind of skilled testing that a bunch of our friends focus on, not these "Hey, did you get the basic things right?"

[08:43] As a programmer, I can find the stupid mistakes, the mistakes that if I just paid more attention or if I was a little bit less distracted or a little bit less stressed, I wouldn't have made those mistakes. I could at least figure out how to find those mistakes without bothering the testers to do it so they could figure out, "If I raise my leg like this and I lean back in my chair, then the system crashes."

[09:07] That's what I need help with. A unit test is not going to find that problem.

Kent: [09:10] That's actually interesting that you bring that up. What would you say for people who do not have dedicated testers, that they are the engineer and the tester at their company?

JB: [09:22] I think the hardest thing there is in that situation it becomes very easy to become addicted to system tests, because it's easy to get started writing system tests. They give you this security blanket feeling, but then the problem is that system tests don't really do a great job of helping you figure out the sources of problems.

[09:43] System tests are slower. System tests are bigger, which means that you need more of them in order to cover the system. What I've been telling programmers over time is that the better I get, the more solid my practice is in having really good, focused, isolated micro tests.

[10:05] Some people like to call them solitary tests now, where we check one thing at a time, and having proper integration tests, where we check the integration between pairs of components instead of trying to worry about what the rest of the world is doing.

[10:18] The better I do that, the more those system tests stop...I stop using them as a primary source of finding problems. I use them instead to confirm that I didn't delude myself with my other work.

[10:31] Those system tests then become the thing that we do to make sure we didn't miss something and not the thing that helps us find problems. I should be able to find most of those problems with the tests that I write for myself.

[10:44] If you are the tester, then I guess you're going to learn about testing. Start by figuring out all the little things. Work on the testing in the small to help you stop making the obvious mistakes.

[11:00] When you don't make so many obvious mistakes, that will free you up to then be interested in questions like, what kinds of tests do I really need to write for this whole system? What does it really mean to test a system as opposed to just a single component, like a single function or a single module?

[11:17] Are there good books out there that I can read about software testing? What am I missing? It's hard to be interested in what you're missing, to be frank, when you're still unable to find any part of the code base with two hands. It's hard to be interested in those bigger areas.

[11:32] Get the little stuff right. Do that well and you'll free up lots of energy to then be interested in the larger questions.

Kent: [11:40] We got into a couple of terms that some people may not be familiar with. It might be good to talk about the return on investment of testing with regard to different levels and what those levels are.

[11:53] Can you talk a little bit about, define a couple terms for us? In software, and particularly in testing I think, we define things very differently. It would maybe be helpful for everybody to understand.

JB: [12:07] I don't think that's just in testing. Broadly speaking, I start with there's two audiences for tests. They have two completely different purposes. I talk about programmer tests and customer tests.

[12:20] Customer tests, those are the tests that should give the customer the warm, fuzzy feeling that we're building the features they've asked for, that we're solving the problems that they need solved, that the business logic made sense.

[12:34] There's a growing feeling, I think, maybe not growing enough, but a growing feeling that we as programmers especially overestimate the chance that a non-programmer customer will ever get a warm, fuzzy feeling from watching a test run.

[12:55] I think they will get that warm, fuzzy feeling by playing around with the test, by playing around with the system and seeing how well it works, or they might get that warm, fuzzy feeling from delegating that work to somebody else and trusting their judgment.

[13:09] By and large, those customer tests, for me, the real value doesn't come from automating those tests. It comes from the act of designing those tests with customers. I mean big C customer in the XP sense.

[13:24] It doesn't have to be a paying customer, but it's those tests that we write that Brian Merrick likes to call examples that merely help us articulate [inaudible] that we're really trying to build. What would the feature look like if it were present now? How would we know that did it?

[13:40] Those tests tend to be end-to-end tests, although not always. They tend to be written entirely from the point of view of an outside observer, although they don't have to be. That's why I don't really find them that valuable as automated tests, because they tend to be the big kinds of integrated tests that I don't like anyway.

[13:58] Some would say they're a scam. I'm sure we'll get back to that. On the other hand, you have programmer tests. The programmer tests are the tests that we programmers writer for ourselves to give us rock-solid confidence that the code does what we believe we asked it to do.

[14:15] I try to spend most of my time writing micro tests that test one thing at a time that make very clear what is the contract between pieces of the system so that I don't have to put everything together to see if it works, so that I know for every component what responsibility do they have? How do they talk to each other? How do I know that when I put these things together, they'll just work?

[14:42] That comes from this whole integrated tests are a scam thing. Rather than talk about the scam first, for me, the medicine, the solution lies in not trying to put everything together, but instead in having a system, a strategy for figuring out do I know how to talk to my neighbors? Do my neighbors implement their contract correctly?

[15:08] I talk about collaboration and contract tests. You have now three kinds of programmer tests, or four, I guess. The first three are the tests that show that this thing behaves correctly on its own. I do some calculations.

[15:22] I give you a result, easy. [inaudible] equals. No problem. Then, there's the collaboration tests. Do I talk to my neighbors correctly? The mock objects tests that people tend to talk about out there, most of the time, they're talking about collaboration tests. They don't even realize that there's another side of the equation.

[15:41] It's great to know that I know that I can talk to my neighbors, but how do I know that my neighbors behave the way that I expect? Every time I write a test and I say, "Stub this method's return 12," I'm making an assumption that that method can return 12. How do I prove that?

[15:59] Anybody who writes collaboration tests eventually reaches the point where they start to ask the question, how do I know that my mock objects match the way the real thing behaves? That's the missing part. The part that people miss is contract tests.

[16:13] Contract tests tell me that when I implement that interface, I follow the rules. The rules are the contract of the interface. This thing in the middle, the interface between client and supplier, which may be a little weird to say in JavaScript because in JavaScript, everything is in interface all the time. That's how dot-typing works.

[16:33] It's even harder in JavaScript to keep the contract straight, because everything is in interface all the time, and contracts are everywhere. It feels weird to write any of that down. At least in Java, C++, C#, there's an interface data type. There's a type for that. It's a first-class concept. There's a natural reminder that we should think about this.

[16:55] Those collaboration tests are where we design the interface and we say, "This is what I want that interface to do." I'm designing the contract. I'm specifying the contract. I'm saying, "This is how I wish the next layer would behave." Then when you implement the contract tests, you're saying, "Oh, and let me show you that I do in fact behave that way."

[17:14] Now, I imagine at every stage of the system, you had collaboration tests that showed that I talked to the next layer correctly. You have contract tests that show that I implemented the next layer correctly. When you put everything together, it just works.

[17:28] Now I say, "It just works," and your audience should hear a little trademark symbol above "It just works," although I guess that's more of a service mark. The idea there is if I do the collaboration and contract tests well, I don't need to rely on system tests to be confident in the code.

[17:48] I get my confidence from the fact that when I put the components together, they'll fit, they'll talk to each other. Nothing totally bizarre will happen. Then I go back to the customer tests, and I run those to try to answer the question, "Oh, and by the way, did I solve the business problem that the customer actually wants solved?"

[18:08] Those programmer tests are all for me. They're for me to know that I wrote code that does what I wanted. That the customer tests are to give me some way of saying, I understood the problem well enough, and I think I built it correctly. Now, let's give it to a customer and see what they say.

[18:25] Have confidence that they won't just spit it back at me and yell, that it'll actually probably be 95, 96, 97 percent of what they wanted.

Kent: [18:35] That was really great, all those. I'm trying to map some of these definitions back to the JavaScript land, because things are a little bit different. Maybe you can help me map some of these things.

[18:50] It sounds like programmer tests that you're talking about, where it's the micro tests, it's a unit test where you're mocking out the dependencies of a particular JavaScript module or something and you're verifying. Also, pure functions are just super easy to test in JavaScript as well. That's the kind of test that we're looking for there. Then the contract testing, you're right.

[19:13] It doesn't really work. Even when you're tacking on a type system on top of JavaScript with Flow or TypeScript, it still isn't quite the same. I think contract testing in JavaScript is more of component integration testing. You don't mock out these dependencies, and you just make sure they talk to each other properly.

JB: [19:34] Actually, I would say not quite. For me, as you say, if you bolt on a type system, like with TypeScript, for example, what you get -- free of charge, more or less -- is what I call contract syntax. That is, you need to have this method. Yes, I have this method.

[19:55] In PHP, Python, JavaScript, whatever, you might need a test for that, or you might just say, "I've been doing it for 10 years. I never get this wrong. It'll be fine. I'll just wait for message not understood at the other end."

[20:09] With something like Java, C#, or with TypeScript, what you get now is a compile time type checker of some kind, some kind of type checker that says, "You said you would have these methods, and you don't. I'm not going to let you connect these things together." That's good, but that's not enough.

[20:23] What's left over is contract semantics. What are the rules of the game? When a failure happens, do I return an either? Do I return a return code? Do I throw an exception? When you give me this input, I give you that kind of output. When you give me this input, I give you that kind of output. If you give me null, here's how I handle it.

[20:46] Those semantics have to do with, when I ask the next layer to do something, do I know how it's going to respond? Those rules of the game aren't just method signatures. For example, I'm implementing some kind of a repository, and I can find stuff. You give me a key, I give you back an object. Obviously, I have something that looks like a look-up table.

[21:14] On the one hand, the contract can be, if I have something, I will give it to you, and if I don't have something, I will return null. I'm not saying that's a good idea, but it's a simple contract for now. That's the semantics, but the semantics means more than that.

[21:32] From the point of view of the client, what really matters is, when I ask you, when I give you a key, and you give me back a value, not null, that's not just a value. That's the value that matches that key. That's the guarantee you're making to me.

[21:46] If I give you a key, and you give me back null, you're giving me back null for only one reason. The only one reason is, you don't have anything that matches that key. You're not representing other kinds of errors. Nothing else weird happened. That to me is really the contract semantics.

[22:00] That's what makes this stuff work, is that we really clearly articulate, what are we expecting, and how do we interpret that? Then I want a contract that says, if I create a repository that has this key mapped to that value, when you look up the key, you should get that value back and not something else.

[22:23] When I don't have a matching value for that key, and you ask for that key, I give you back null, and I don't throw an exception. I give you something else. That contract test is the piece that most people are missing.

[22:33] It allows me to check that my implementation matches the client's expectations specifically without connecting the client and the supplier together. The whole point is, the supplier doesn't care where the questions are coming from.

[22:49] I shouldn't have to connect those two things together to see if they work. I should be able to just say, "Look, clients are going to ask these kinds of questions. Let's make sure that you give them the answers they expect."

[22:59] If you do, then at the entry point of my system, or wherever I have my framework extension points, when I do finally decide that implementation has to talk to that implementation, they will just work, because this guy respects the contract that this guy's expecting. I don't need a system test to feel confident about that. For me, that's the point.

Kent: [23:22] That's interesting. You can feel confident, because you have a contract test on both sides. How do you automate the confidence that the contract test is the same, and that they're both testing the same contract?

JB: [23:37] You don't. The client is making assumptions about the contract, and the supplier is, if you will -- proving is not quite the right word -- is demonstrating that they actually fulfill that contract.

[23:53] As far as I know, there is no way yet to automate the correspondence between the collaboration test and the contract test. Is one of those open areas where, when I first say that, some programmers slump back in their chair and say, "Well, all you did was move the problem around." Maybe.

[24:10] I think what I did is, I provided a more systematic solution to the problem. For example, let's say I put two things together and they don't work. Some system test finds a problem or some tester is poking around and finds some unexpected behavior. How did that happen?

[24:28] It happens because this guy has a collaboration test that doesn't match this contract test, this guy has a collaboration test missing, or, this guy has a contract test missing. The good news is it's one of those three things.

[24:45] I suppose it's possible that, inside the object, they're doing something really weird, but that's pretty rare. Normally, it's one of those three. Either I'm missing something, I'm missing something, or we disagree. The disagreeing, this, the good news is that gives me something very specific to look for.

[25:02] I don't just wander around the code base blind, hoping to hit upon the problem. I know exactly where to look. I can go, layer by layer, and check, does this collaboration test have a matching test? Yep. Go to the next layer. I can keep going. Yeah, it might feel a little tedious and maybe even a bit boring.

[25:23] I don't know about you, if I'm trying to fix a problem in a software system, I like boring. I'm not into debugging because I like drama. I want to find a problem, figure out what the problem was, fix it, figure out why I let the problem, stop that from happening again, and go on with my day.

[25:43] That's part of what this collaboration and contract tests idea gives me. It gives me a very systematic way to go layer by layer and find a problem. There are shortcuts, but literally, you can do that. Start at the very top of the application and check, this is the collaboration test that matches that path. Is there a contract test to it? Yes. The problem might be in the next layer.

[26:04] Keep going until you find it, and then you make them fit. There is a group of folks in Israel who are working on this problem. I just spoke with them a week or two weeks ago. [inaudible] computer to make that correspondence for.

[26:22] Could I have some red, flashing light that goes off -- I guess goes on -- that flashes when I change a collaboration test and I forget to change a contract test, or when I add a contract test and I don't have the corresponding collaboration test? Could the computer warn me that I just made a mistake?

[26:38] As far as I know, so far, no. That's why we have things like Chado in JavaScript land or Pact in Ruby land. What they're really trying to do is give you that contract syntax thing, the same thing that a compile-time type-checker gives you. I implement this interface, but I didn't actually provide that method. That's the easy part, really.

[27:01] There's a group in Israel that are trying to come up with a way. I believe they're doing it in TypeScript, where you can mark tests as [inaudible] . Then, if you make a change in one of them, you get a warning about the other, which sound interesting.

[27:17] I'm not 100 percent sure that it's worth the trouble, but it's the kind of thing where, if they figure it out, it's a game-changer. It's the kind of thing that then will make it very easy to apply this approach and not worry about getting it wrong.

[27:34] You could throw paint against the wall and eventually you might paint most of the wall, but until you go up to the wall with a brush, you'll never get the corners. That's what it's like when we try to use system tests to find these problems.

[27:47] We might find 60 percent of them, 50 percent of them, or 70 percent of them, but we'll never really get the edges until we go there with the surgical tools, to mix the metaphor. That's what these collaboration and contract tests are for. They're the surgical tool that allows me to say, "Yeah, this layer has the [inaudible] layer does mess with that."

Kent: [28:09] I like that metaphor of throwing a bucket of paint against the wall. We are over time. Sorry. No, I'm not sorry.

JB: [28:21] I'm not worried.

Kent: [28:22] This was fantastic. It was awesome, so [laughs] why am I saying sorry? Thank you very much for giving me some of your time, and the people watching. I like to end these with a bit of homework.

[28:36] If there was one thing that our viewers could do to make their code more testable or improve their test base, in general, what would that piece of homework be?

JB: [28:48] One of the things that I notice a lot is we get tests with really long set-up. It makes clients of mine nervous, which I understand. I don't like it either. It seems like I'm doing an awful lot of work. I am copying and pasting a whole bunch of pointless details when all I'm interested in is this thing down here. There's two things I want to say about that if you'll let me.

Kent: [29:09] Yeah, sure.

JB: [29:10] The first one is that's a clue. The fact that you are copying and pasting details that you don't care about is a clue. One bit of homework, the harder homework if you will, the fuzzier homework, is look for situations where your test includes details that don't matter for the thing that you're trying to check.

[29:31] Ask yourself why is that? Why do I need to run this stuff and set up these mocks, set up this configuration setting, or whatever when really, all I want to do is put input here and check the output there? If I can't make it that easy, why not?

[29:46] I'm suggesting that that general idea points to weaknesses in the production code design. There's a specific case of this that see repeatedly. That is when the function that you're testing knows too much about where to get its inputs from.

[30:04] I play this game with my clients. I did it, literally, yesterday. I asked a room of programmers, "What is the point of having parameters in the function? Why do functions have parameters?" It's one of those questions that seem so simple. Everybody assumes that everybody else has the answer and they're the only who don't know, so they get very nervous about answering.

[30:28] I try very hard to say, "Look, folks. You'd be surprised how many people can't answer this question. Calm down. It's not a trick, I promise. Why do functions have parameters?" Eventually, we're trying to get them to say, "So that I can work with any input value. I don't care what the input value is."

[30:44] I said, "Exactly. In particular, functions have parameters so that you don't know where your input comes from. Every time I try to test something that has to go over here and grab a piece of information and go over here and grab a piece of information and go over here to grab a piece of information, what we're doing is we're writing functions that know where their input comes from.

[31:04] Don't do that. Instead, that's a detail that should be up the call stack one level. Let the person above you worry about where the details come from, or where the input comes from. Instead, just say, "Look, if I need five pieces of information to do my job, then I have five parameters. Give me five parameters."

[31:24] This is a very functional programming approach, the idea that we ruthlessly and relentlessly write functions that don't know where their input comes from. They're really easy to test, because I can just say, I know where the input comes from. My tests, I have 47 tests for all the combinations of five parameters that are interesting.

[31:44] I will push one level up the call stack worrying about, "Well, this level comes from over here and this part comes from over here and this part comes from over here." If you do that, your design will get better in ways that you won't have imagined. I'll leave that as an exercise to the viewer.

Kent: [31:59] Thank you. Last question. Is there anyone or anything that you'd like to give a particular shout out to? It doesn't have to be related to testing. It's just anything that you want to say, pretty much.

JB: [32:12] This year marks my 20th year in the extreme programming community.

[32:19] I want to give a shout out to the old XP folks that got tired of watching Agile become, I don't know what adjective I want to use, so maybe I won't use one, but I think people will understand, who stood back and watched Agile become this tortured mess and who said, "You know what? I don't want to play with you folks anymore. You guys are nuts. I don't know what happened to you. Good luck."

[32:50] They're the people who we hung out with in the old days in the Yahoo Groups, but that I don't get a chance to talk to anymore. If by chance somebody out there knows one of those folks, or if one of them happens to watch this, hey, we remember you and you're some of the giants whose shoulders we stand on. Don't forget it.

Kent: [33:13] [laughs] Thank you so much, JB. I really appreciate your time. It looks like it's getting dark outside for you.

JB: [33:19] It is.

Kent: [33:19] Have a good evening and we'll see everybody later.

JB: [33:23] Thanks very much, folks.

Kent: [33:23] Bye.

00:29:19,430 --> 00:29:21,170
bit of homework, the harder 

903
00:29:21,170 --> 00:29:22,470
homework if you will, the 

904
00:29:22,600 --> 00:29:24,200
fuzzier homework, is look for 

905
00:29:24,200 --> 00:29:25,710
situations where your test 

906
00:29:26,160 --> 00:29:27,500
includes details that don't 

907
00:29:27,800 --> 00:29:29,680
matter for the thing that you're 

908
00:29:29,680 --> 00:29:32,390
trying to check. Ask yourself 

909
00:29:32,730 --> 00:29:34,250
why is that? Why do I need to 

910
00:29:34,250 --> 00:29:35,670
run this stuff and set up these 

911
00:29:35,700 --> 00:29:37,240
mocks, set up this configuration 

912
00:29:37,240 --> 00:29:39,070
setting, or whatever when really, 

913
00:29:39,070 --> 00:29:40,690
all I want to do is put input 

914
00:29:40,730 --> 00:29:42,140
here and check the output there? 

915
00:29:42,940 --> 00:29:44,610
If I can't make it that easy, 

916
00:29:44,920 --> 00:29:47,920
why not? I'm suggesting that 

917
00:29:47,940 --> 00:29:50,510
that general idea points to 

918
00:29:50,630 --> 00:29:51,720
weaknesses in the production 

919
00:29:51,720 --> 00:29:53,760
code design. There's a specific 

920
00:29:53,760 --> 00:29:55,930
case of this that see repeatedly. 

921
00:29:56,760 --> 00:30:00,550
That is when the function that 

922
00:30:00,550 --> 00:30:02,260
you're testing knows too much 

923
00:30:02,260 --> 00:30:03,400
about where to get its inputs 

924
00:30:04,190 --> 00:30:06,410
from. I play this game with my 

925
00:30:06,410 --> 00:30:08,120
clients. I did it, literally, 

926
00:30:08,120 --> 00:30:10,150
yesterday. I asked a room of 

927
00:30:10,190 --> 00:30:13,360
programmers, "What is the point 

928
00:30:13,360 --> 00:30:14,420
of having parameters in the 

929
00:30:14,420 --> 00:30:17,350
function? Why do functions have 

930
00:30:18,110 --> 00:30:20,130
parameters?" It's one of those 

931
00:30:20,130 --> 00:30:21,880
questions that seem so simple. 

932
00:30:22,420 --> 00:30:24,230
Everybody assumes that everybody 

933
00:30:24,230 --> 00:30:25,520
else has the answer and they're 

934
00:30:25,720 --> 00:30:27,250
the only who don't know, so they 

935
00:30:27,250 --> 00:30:28,570
get very nervous about answering. 

936
00:30:28,800 --> 00:30:30,200
I try very hard to say, "Look, 

937
00:30:30,200 --> 00:30:31,450
folks. You'd be surprised how 

938
00:30:31,450 --> 00:30:32,480
many people can't answer this 

939
00:30:32,480 --> 00:30:34,420
question. Calm down. It's not a 

940
00:30:34,420 --> 00:30:35,910
trick, I promise. Why do 

941
00:30:35,910 --> 00:30:37,030
functions have parameters?" 

942
00:30:37,340 --> 00:30:38,520
Eventually, we're trying to get 

943
00:30:38,520 --> 00:30:41,130
them to say, "So that I can work 

944
00:30:41,130 --> 00:30:42,620
with any input value. I don't 

945
00:30:42,620 --> 00:30:44,130
care what the input value is." 

946
00:30:44,320 --> 00:30:46,490
I said, "Exactly. In particular, 

947
00:30:47,020 --> 00:30:48,320
functions have parameters so 

948
00:30:48,320 --> 00:30:49,690
that you don't know where your 

949
00:30:49,690 --> 00:30:52,320
input comes from. Every time I 

950
00:30:52,320 --> 00:30:53,570
try to test something that has 

951
00:30:53,570 --> 00:30:55,110
to go over here and grab a piece 

952
00:30:55,400 --> 00:30:56,650
of information and go over here 

953
00:30:56,650 --> 00:30:57,780
and grab a piece of information 

954
00:30:57,780 --> 00:30:59,040
and go over here to grab a piece 

955
00:30:59,040 --> 00:31:01,120
of information, what we're doing 

956
00:31:01,120 --> 00:31:02,660
is we're writing functions that 

957
00:31:02,710 --> 00:31:04,300
know where their input comes 

958
00:31:04,300 --> 00:31:06,840
from. Don't do that. Instead, 

959
00:31:07,410 --> 00:31:09,370
that's a detail that should be 

960
00:31:09,410 --> 00:31:11,430
up the call stack one level. Let 

961
00:31:11,980 --> 00:31:13,970
the person above you worry about 

962
00:31:13,970 --> 00:31:15,200
where the details come from, or 

963
00:31:15,200 --> 00:31:16,050
where the input comes from. 

964
00:31:16,350 --> 00:31:17,690
Instead, just say, "Look, if I 

965
00:31:17,690 --> 00:31:19,080
need five pieces of information 

966
00:31:19,080 --> 00:31:21,210
to do my job, then I have five 

967
00:31:21,260 --> 00:31:22,740
parameters. Give me five 

968
00:31:22,740 --> 00:31:25,130
parameters." This is a very 

969
00:31:25,170 --> 00:31:26,650
functional programming approach, 

970
00:31:27,070 --> 00:31:30,380
the idea that we ruthlessly and 

971
00:31:30,380 --> 00:31:32,280
relentlessly write functions 

972
00:31:32,400 --> 00:31:33,300
that don't know where their 

973
00:31:33,300 --> 00:31:35,460
input comes from. They're really 

974
00:31:35,460 --> 00:31:37,120
easy to test, because I can just 

975
00:31:37,120 --> 00:31:38,980
say, I know where the input 

976
00:31:38,980 --> 00:31:41,190
comes from. My tests, I have 47 

977
00:31:41,190 --> 00:31:42,760
tests for all the combinations 

978
00:31:42,760 --> 00:31:43,740
of five parameters that are 

979
00:31:43,740 --> 00:31:45,740
interesting. I will push one 

980
00:31:45,740 --> 00:31:47,170
level up the call stack worrying 

981
00:31:47,170 --> 00:31:48,150
about, "Well, this level comes 

982
00:31:48,150 --> 00:31:48,920
from over here and this part 

983
00:31:48,980 --> 00:31:49,810
comes from over here and this 

984
00:31:49,810 --> 00:31:51,540
part comes from over here." If 

985
00:31:51,540 --> 00:31:53,820
you do that, your design will 

986
00:31:53,820 --> 00:31:55,270
get better in ways that you 

987
00:31:55,270 --> 00:31:56,910
won't have imagined. I'll leave 

988
00:31:56,910 --> 00:31:59,600
that as an exercise to the 

989
00:31:59,600 --> 00:31:59,900
viewer. 

990
00:31:59,950 --> 00:32:03,140
Thank you. Last question. Is 

991
00:32:03,140 --> 00:32:04,810
there anyone or anything that 

992
00:32:04,810 --> 00:32:05,920
you'd like to give a particular 

993
00:32:05,920 --> 00:32:07,140
shout out to? It doesn't have to 

994
00:32:07,140 --> 00:32:08,490
be related to testing. It's just 

995
00:32:08,490 --> 00:32:09,850
anything that you want to say, 

996
00:32:09,920 --> 00:32:10,430
pretty much. 

997
00:32:12,200 --> 00:32:16,230
This year marks my 20th year in 

998
00:32:16,230 --> 00:32:18,260
the extreme programming 

999
00:32:18,260 --> 00:32:21,880
community. I want to give a 

1000
00:32:21,880 --> 00:32:25,120
shout out to the old XP folks 

1001
00:32:25,400 --> 00:32:29,640
that got tired of watching Agile 

1002
00:32:29,640 --> 00:32:32,860
become, I don't know what 

1003
00:32:32,860 --> 00:32:34,190
adjective I want to use, so 

1004
00:32:34,190 --> 00:32:35,390
maybe I won't use one, but I 

1005
00:32:35,450 --> 00:32:36,680
think people will understand, 

1006
00:32:37,970 --> 00:32:40,300
who stood back and watched Agile 

1007
00:32:40,990 --> 00:32:44,100
become this tortured mess and 

1008
00:32:44,100 --> 00:32:44,990
who said, "You know what? I 

1009
00:32:44,990 --> 00:32:45,630
don't want to play with you 

1010
00:32:45,670 --> 00:32:47,790
folks anymore. You guys are nuts. 

1011
00:32:47,820 --> 00:32:48,670
I don't know what happened to 

1012
00:32:48,670 --> 00:32:51,210
you. Good luck." They're the 

1013
00:32:51,210 --> 00:32:53,940
people who we hung out with in 

1014
00:32:53,940 --> 00:32:56,560
the old days in the Yahoo Groups, 

1015
00:32:56,760 --> 00:32:57,810
but that I don't get a chance to 

1016
00:32:57,810 --> 00:32:59,540
talk to anymore. If by chance 

1017
00:32:59,540 --> 00:33:01,380
somebody out there knows one of 

1018
00:33:01,380 --> 00:33:02,690
those folks, or if one of them 

1019
00:33:02,690 --> 00:33:04,550
happens to watch this, hey, we 

1020
00:33:04,550 --> 00:33:07,820
remember you and you're some of 

1021
00:33:07,820 --> 00:33:09,450
the giants whose shoulders we 

1022
00:33:09,450 --> 00:33:13,490
stand on. Don't forget it. 

1023
00:33:13,490 --> 00:33:14,600
 Thank you so much, JB. 

1024
00:33:14,850 --> 00:33:16,130
I really appreciate your time. 

1025
00:33:16,390 --> 00:33:17,430
It looks like it's getting dark 

1026
00:33:17,430 --> 00:33:18,240
outside for you. 

1027
00:33:19,130 --> 00:33:19,500
It is. 

1028
00:33:19,890 --> 00:33:21,410
Have a good evening and we'll 

1029
00:33:21,410 --> 00:33:22,510
see everybody later. 

1030
00:33:23,000 --> 00:33:23,910
Thanks very much, folks. 

1031
00:33:23,985 --> 00:33:24,310
Bye.

fields.title: Visual regression testing with Angie Jones
fields.summary: Angie Jones is a Senior Developer Advocate who specializes in test automation strategies and techniques. She shares her wealth of knowledge by speaking and teaching at software conferences all over the world.
fields.transcript:
Kent C. Dodds: [00:00] Hey, everybody, it's Kent again. I am joined by the wonderful Angie Jones. Hello, Angie

Angie Jones: [00:07] Hello, Kent.

Kent: [00:09] Angie is a super nice person. I just barely met her, but so far, she's been super nice [laughs] and recommended highly by several people to me on Twitter and other places. Excited to chat with Angie.

[00:23] Angie, could you give us an intro to yourself just for us to get to know you a little bit, what you care about, what your interest in the software world is, what you're all about?

Kent: [00:37] I'm a developer advocate at Applitools, which is a visual validation company. I specialize in test automation. I've been doing test automation for quite a while, about 15 years. I've done it for a lot of cool companies, just as IBM and Twitter.

[00:59] I did a couple of years in development as well, but found my way back to testing, just because I love it so much. It's definitely where my passion is. I do a lot of speaking and teaching about test automation all over the world, so always on the go, trying to spread the gospel of test automation.

Kent: [01:26] That sounds awesome, 15 years of testing experience. You have a lot of experience with automated testing.

Angie: [01:35] Yep.

Kent: [01:36] You went back to development and then you just came back to testing. What is it that draws you toward testing? Why do you care so much about testing?

Angie: [01:45] I started out in the test automation role. That was my first job out of school. After graduation, I didn't know anything about testing or test automation. I really want to go into development. That's all I knew, "I want to code."

[02:06] My first job was an automation job. I enjoyed it, but I still, in the back of my mind, was thinking I wanted to do development. After a couple of years in automation, I finally got the opportunity to go into development, and I dreaded it so much.

[02:23] It was so different. We think coding is coding, but in the development roles, the assignments that I would get would be very scoped to a specific feature, "Build this widget," or something like that. In test automation, I had this bigger worldview of the product.

[02:41] I understood all the ins and outs and how they interacted with each other. Even in the development piece, though, I was able to architect solutions. I felt like I was flexing my development muscles a whole lot more doing test automation development versus the production development that I was doing.

[03:02] Not to mention the whole stress and pressure of the deadlines of production and development. It just wasn't as fun. I did about two years in development and then moved back into test automation. That's where I've been ever since, and that's where I belong, I believe.

Kent: [03:26] I'm curious, as a primarily a QA engineer or automated tester, what do you find is the most difficult part of the transition from the developer who writes the product to transition that off onto you as someone who tests the product?

[03:45] Let's say that you and I are working on a product together. I build out this feature and I hand that feature off to you before it's shipped for you to write some automated tests. Is there anything in that process that you've experienced that was hard and maybe some things you could talk about to make that process easier?

Kent: [04:06] I think that it's crucial to make sure development understands how to make their applications testable. Just developing a feature and handing it off seems OK in theory, but I've been in a lot of situations where you try to automate against it, and it's a nightmare.

[04:27] It takes so long to do because the app isn't testable. There aren't those plug-ins that help you along the way. For example, the one that's really obvious is if you're doing UI development, having IDs or different locators to allow for test automation to be able to access the elements.

[04:48] Not having those might not seem like a big deal, but it definitely slows down your automation development when that person has to go and try to craft CSS selectors or XPath selectors to be able to find a nice, stable handle to that where an ID could have been put there and solved the problem.

[05:11] I like to work with developers before they hand me the feature, almost in the design phase of it. I talk about doing a little meeting with them and essentially mock up what the UI is going to look like after this feature is developed.

[05:31] During that mock-up, we can start thinking about how to embed the testability in there as well, and even create this little contract between us and start identifying the elements right there. "OK, let's call this...This ID's name will be this and this ID's name will be that. I'll need these, these, these."

[05:51] I can leave that meeting before development has even started, and I have a clear visual of what it is that I want to automate. I can actually go to my desk and start coding the test right then and there because I have the IDs to these elements already. I have a visual of how the scenario will flow. Once they deliver that feature to me, it's a matter of hit and play and letting it go.

Kent: [06:19] That's great advice. In the course that I'm releasing, I really push hard the idea of these testing libraries that I've developed. One of the ways that you select items to make assertions on is via a data test ID attribute.

[06:44] A lot of times when I tell people to use these, the optimal is to get an input by its label text, and there are utilities for that because that's what the user's going to be doing. They're going to look for the username field based off of the text that says username. Sometimes, there's not a good way to select a certain element.

[07:10] As a backup, we have this utility for getting items by their test ID. A lot of people will ask me about that and say, "Now we're shipping extra stuff to the end user that they don't need." There are ways to get rid of that. We've got Babel plug-ins and stuff to remove that from your source code before you ship.

[07:36] I tell them, "No, this is really useful for testing in production or smoke testing." I say leave it in because it makes things a lot easier for automated testing of your deployed application. It's good to hear that because that validates what I've been telling people as well.

[07:59] Let's talk a little bit about your company, Applitools, and what you're all about. In my course, I have this thing called the Testing Trophy. You start out with static code analysis, with linters and static typing tools, and then you have a layer of unit tests, a bigger layer of integration tests, a layer of end-to-end tests.

[08:25] There's one layer that I don't really cover, and that is what Applitools does, which is visual regression testing. Can you talk a little bit about that and maybe how visual regression testing differs from these other forms?

Angie: [08:39] Visual regression testing is fairly new to this scene when we think about test automation. What I love about it is it's not a replacement for any of the testing that we already do. It's something that can be added in addition to and give you additional coverage of your validations.

[09:03] What it does is it compares images. Once you run your test one time and you're sure of the results there, you can save this as a baseline and essentially say, "This is what my app should look like going forward." When you run your regression test, it's comparing against that as well.

[09:22] You can have all of your functional testing done as well, plus it'll make sure that they look and feel is correct. If we think about automated tests, there was a scenario I saw on Twitter. Someone made the comment, "Why don't people test their apps better?" or, "Why don't they do cross-router testing?"

[09:43] The picture that was shown was where you save your credit card in this application. There was a yes label, a no label, two radio buttons next to it, and then the button to actually save. The labels were both shifted to the left so the correct label wasn't on the correct radio button. You didn't know which one was yes and which one was no.

[10:11] This is something that if you automated against this, this would totally pass because all of the functionality is there. The labels are there? Yep, they exist. The radio buttons are there? Yep, they exist. If you're selecting the radio button by its ID, that's just fine, and you click the button. That test can be passing and not alerting anyone that something is wrong, visually.

[10:37] That's where the importance of this whole visual validation comes in. That's just one case. There's so many cases where we see...We call it GUIgonewrong hashtag. We see this all over the place, and it's not just your mom-and-pop software development shop. This is everyone. We see this from Amazon. We see this from Facebook, from Microsoft, from Twitter.

[11:02] It's really a big deal and it's something that we can't rely on humans to validate every screen of the application. It looks like we can't even really rely on our functional validation so it's really cool to add this additional component.

[11:18] As I've been using it more and more, I find that even helps me with my functional validations as well. If I had something like a shopping cart, for example, and I wanted to verify that some item was in the shopping cart, I think about the list of assertions that I have to write against that.

[11:39] I have to make sure that that product's title was right, its quantity is right, its subtotal is right, its final total is right, that the remove button is there. There's so many assertions that I would have to write programmatically. Whereas, I can use visual validation so it doesn't have to be the whole screen. You can even assert on just a portion of the screen.

[12:01] If I just selected the cart element and said just validate on this, I could have one assertion written against all of those things. It's like the old saying, a picture...What does it say? A picture's worth a thousand words. A picture's worth a thousand verifications.

Kent: [12:21] I like that a lot. I feel like the Applitools, as you said, it doesn't really necessarily replace the other forms of testing, but it can definitely enhance things. Right now, most people are probably writing more focused assertions and not asserting some things that maybe they should, like that the text that says total is there.

[12:47] Maybe they break something and that text goes away, and all they're validating is the actual total amount or something so they miss out. I don't blame them. Can you imagine? Having to, just like what you said, manually verify all those things would be kind of silly.

[13:09] In my course, I show how to do end-to-end testing with Cypress. I feel like using Applitools in connection with the tests you already have written in Cypress would be a pretty good approach. From what I understand, Applitools is working on integrating well with Cypress. Can you talk about that at all? Are you familiar with the Cypress tool?

Angie: [13:35] Definitely. We're releasing that SDK for Cypress. It's definitely a great compliment for that tool as well. We support just about all of the major automation tools out there so it's really cool. You don't have to feel like you're replacing something or you have to go with one certain library in order to use it.

[13:58] We have so many SDKs out there. Whatever is your cup of tea, just add visual validation to it.

Kent: [14:08] Very cool. Something to look into. When I talked with some other Applitools folks a while ago, they said they were currently working on making Applitools work with Cypress and with Jest.

[14:22] Maybe you can correct me or maybe you can explain a little bit about how Applitools works. I think a question that a lot of people would ask is, "How does this change my workflow? Can I only run my tests inside of the Applitools cloud or can I still run my tests locally or on my own CI?" and then, "How do I approve when a visual change is actually a desired change?"

[14:56] Can you talk a little bit about technically how it works, and then how it impacts my workflow?

Angie: [15:01] Sure. Technically, we differ from a lot of the other visual validation tools on the market. Most of those do a pixel-by-pixel comparison. We've found that that's really problematic, especially when you're trying to run across multiple browsers or machines.

[15:24] Different browsers might render things a little bit differently. The white space and the margins might be different. The fonts might be slightly different. The color, the hue of it, one might be deeper on a certain browser or a certain machine.

[15:43] Using a pixel-by-pixel comparison would cause a lot of failures that aren't really things that we care about as human beings. What we use at Applitools is we use AI to be able to compare images just like a human eye would. Things that are irrelevant to a human eye, we discard those and only flag the errors that people care about.

[16:16] Like I said, it's not just a page comparison. You can do an entire page comparison or you can scope it down to specific elements or components on a page. In addition to that, you might want to do a layout flow.

[16:31] I used to work at Twitter, and it was really difficult to automate against some of that because the tweets might be different. It wasn't like you can rely on a certain tweet being there without staging this data.

[16:44] With Applitools, you can use this for layout verification as well. I know what a tweet's layout should be. I know how the page should look. I should have an ad here. In the tweet, I should have an avatar, a name, a timestamp, and all of these different things. I don't care what the actual text of it says, only that it's formatted correctly.

[17:11] Applitools offers all of that. As far as integrating it into your existing system, it's fairly simple. It's just a dependency. If you're using Maven or something, you just add it to your pom file. It's a couple of lines of code, maybe eight at max, to be able to get it going and insert it into your test.

[17:40] We'll store your images if that's what you want. We have our cloud, but there are a lot of people -- we have banks and stuff that are our customers -- who absolutely have to have their information on premise. We also support that as well.

[17:59] As far as updating your baselines and things, if your tests were to fail, we have a dashboard where you can easily go to the dashboard and you can see what failed, see if you need to update your baseline.

[18:15] Maybe the application changed in the way that you were expecting it to. You say, "Yeah, OK. That's the new baseline, cool." You don't have to go back to your test and start updating test and trying to figure out what's the new ID and all of that jazz. You just say, "Oh, yeah, cool. Yep, that looks right. Update it."

[18:35] If it's not what you wanted, it's a problem, you've identified a bug, you can go ahead and signal that as a bug. We have a whole team flow where you can essentially open a Jira. We integrate with Jira. You can open a Jira ticket right there from the dashboard.

[18:53] You can assign this to someone else to look at, to a developer, another automation engineer, or tester or someone if you just want additional eyes on it. It's a really cool flow, pretty seamless really. It doesn't add a whole lot of overhead to your existing flow.

[19:12] There are some things to be aware of. If your app is constantly changing, for example, then doing an entire page, it's as your app is being updated. There are ways around it just using a different method. Like I said, you can do it at a component level or you can do the layout. Just knowing how to structure it so that it makes sense for whatever you're doing, your context, is key.

Kent: [19:41] That sound great. I got a demo of the Applitools dashboard and I thought it was really impressive. The AI nature, artificial intelligence nature of this tool is what makes it useful.

[19:55] I remember trying to use an open source solution called Huxley several years ago. It was constantly breaking, so you lose all trust in the tool. It's almost like having no test at all because it was just a frustrating experience, not very fun.

[20:16] I think we're about ready to wrap up here a little bit. Is there anything else that you want to mention about Applitools or visual regression testing, in general?

Angie: [20:27] I think that a lot of people think of this as, "That's a pretty cool nice-to-have." As we are developing across so many different platforms now, mobile and all of the different devices, OSs, and browsers on Web and all of this, it's becoming more imperative to add this to your toolkit.

[20:57] I definitely think people should check it out at least. Even if it's not the fit for your current project, it's always nice to know what's out there and what's available to add to your tool belt and figure out when you need to pull it out.

Kent: [21:14] I think the fact that it doesn't replace any of your existing tools and integrates well with the existing tools is pretty compelling. One thing that I think would be a good idea to mention is that the Cypress testing tool, it's really amazing, but there's one criticism that I hear a lot about it, that it doesn't currently support any other browsers other than Chrome.

Angie: [21:39] Yeah.

Kent: [21:40] That's going to change. They have announced plans to even support Internet Explorer 11.

Angie: [21:47] Oh, wow.

Kent: [21:48] That's amazing. One thing that is really compelling about Applitools is that you don't have to run your tests in all of these browsers for Applitools to run its visual assertion.

[22:03] You can correct me if I'm wrong, but from what I understand, when I issue that Cypress command from the Applitools integration SDK thing, what it's going to do is it'll take a snapshot of the dom and all the CSS that's applied on the page, everything.

[22:20] It will send it to Applitools, which will then load that all up into the various browsers to take the visual snapshot. Even though the test itself is running in Chrome, you can get the visual assertion in all browsers, mobile, whatever. Is that correct, Angie?

Angie: [22:40] That is one of our latest features. That should be out very soon. It's going to be a game-changer. Like you said, it'll be a whole lot faster. You don't have to have that manual maintenance of, "Let me run my test and get my baselines on all of these different browsers."

[23:01] Just like you said, it'll be able to grab the dom, and then render it itself and take care of all of that stuff for you. Pretty excited about that.

Kent: [23:12] That's an incredibly compelling situation. Lots of people's concerns with cross-browser support is in JavaScript support and things, but I feel like we've got a pretty good handle on that with the polyfill tools that we have now.

[23:30] Mostly, the concerns with cross-browser support is the way things look. If I can just run my test one time, then send it to Applitools, and it will apply that on tons of browsers, mobile, and everything, then I can get a lot more confidence without having to take all the time to actually run those test in all the different browsers.

[23:53] That's pretty compelling, definitely something I recommend people give a look at.

Angie: [23:58] Yep.

Kent: [24:00] As we wrap up there, Angie, if you could give the viewers one thing to do to improve their testing, whether it be visual regression, just making more testable code, or whatever it is, what would you tell people to do? What's our homework?

Angie: [24:17] The homework would be to take a step back. Get off of the keyboard for a moment and think about what your strategy is. What is your end goal and why are you even automating? I see a lot of teams fail or get lost in actually coding anything that they can, all of the tests.

[24:38] This is where they find themselves in a bind. Now they're blindly adding things to their regression suite. They're slowing down their CI pipelines. They're adding additional maintenance where it doesn't need to be. Just what is it that you want your test to be able to tell you, and then automate according to that.

Kent: [24:59] I think that's really good advice for lots of things, to take a step back and reevaluate. Thank you for that.

[25:08] One last thing. Is there anyone or anything in particular that you'd like to give a shout-out to? It doesn't have to be related to testing. It can be if you want. Anything in particular you'd like to tell everybody about?

Angie: [25:23] I would like to give a shout-out to the TestBash Conference. Have you heard of this conference, Kent?

Kent: [25:31] No, I haven't. It's Test Badge?

Angie: [25:32] It's amazing, TestBash. It's developed by Ministry of Testing. They do them all over the world. We're having one in San Francisco in early November. I just did one last month. I've done a couple of these conferences, and they are absolutely amazing.

[25:51] They do such a great job of incorporating community. It's not just about the speakers. Everyone in attendance contributes to this feeling of community. Everyone leaves there so revived. I was there last week and got replenished, ready to go, and excited. It does that for so many people so shout-out to TestBash.

Kent: [26:16] Awesome. Thank you so much. That sounds like a fun conference. Thank you for all of your expertise and chatting with me, Angie. You've taken 15 years of experience and you have so much knowledge, and so I appreciate you sharing that with me and the viewers. I'm sure this was helpful for a lot of people, so thank you.

Angie: [26:38] Great. Thanks for having me.

Kent: [26:40] Awesome. See you, everybody.

Angie: [26:43] Bye-bye.

fields.title: What's changed in Install, Configure, and Script Cypress for JavaScript Web Applications
fields.transcript:
Kent C. Dodds: [0:00] Hi there. Pardon the interruption. I just want to tell you about one change in the code since the time that I recorded the videos.

[0:07] It's a pretty minimal change, but it has a pretty significant impacts on the videos, and so until I get time to get around to updating the videos themselves, I wanted to just throw this in here so that you aren't confused about the latest versions of some of the things.

[0:21] All that I changed here was I updated the packages for Cypress Testing Library, Cypress itself, as well as the ESLint Plugin for Cypress. The impact of these changes is small.

[0:32] In particular, we don't need to change anything with the way that we're using Cypress. That's still working. The impact of the change is just with Cypress Testing Library, and I'll show you that here right now.

[0:43] As we go through the videos, you'll see me do stuff like cy.visit(), and then findByText and then click and then findByText again and then click, and just chaining those commands like that.

[0:52] That's no longer supported with the latest version of Cypress Testing Library and so instead you say cy and then findByText and then click. Then when you're done with that element that you are interacting with, you do another cy command, rather than chaining it continuously.

[1:07] That's it. That's really the only change. Actually, there is one other change. That is before, if you wanted to verify that something did not exist in the document, you had to switch from a findBy to a queryBy.

[1:20] We've now consolidated those and made it a lot simpler. Now you only ever use findBy when you're using Cypress Testing Library. Then it just works, because it chains together with the should('not.exist') assertion that you have there.

[1:34] Other than that, everything in the videos is exactly right. You can continue and follow through with the videos. The only difference is we're no longer allowed to do this chaining.

[1:46] I hope that makes a lot of sense to you. I'm excited for you to learn how to use this amazing tool to write end-to-end tests for your applications. Have a good time. We'll see you around the Internet.

everything in the videos is

exactly right. You can continue

and follow through with the

videos. The only difference is

we're no longer allowed to do

this chaining. I hope that

makes a lot of sense to you. I'm

excited for you to learn how to

end-to-end tests for your

applications. Have a good time.

We'll see you around the

00:01:58,205 --> 00:01:58,530
Internet.

fields.title: What's changed in Test React Components with Jest and React Testing Library
fields.transcript:
Kent C. Dodds: [0:00] Hi, friends. Sorry for the interruption. I wanted to give you a little bit of an update on some of the material here. There are a couple of minor changes that have a pretty significant impact on the videos that you're about to watch. Hopefully, it's not super confusing or anything. They're pretty small details, but they do make a big impact on the videos.

[0:22] I haven't been able to get around to updating the videos as they are right now, but I didn't want you to have to wait for how long it would take me to get these videos updated before you get an understanding of what these changes are.

[0:35] I just want to give you a quick rundown of what the changes are that you can expect as you watch through these videos, and it shouldn't impact your understanding or the things that you're trying to learn.

[0:46] Here we are in the react-testing-library-course repo. It looks like a lot of changes, but I promise you, it's just a handful of relatively minor and small things, and I just want to call them out really quick.

[1:01] The first one is, here, I'm using the HTTP Jest mock as an example. You'll notice that we have render, where we're rendering different elements and components and things. With this, we no longer are going to accept a return value here.

[1:18] We recommend that you use screen, which is a new export out of React Testing Library, which will give you access to all of the different queries that you need to make. Now it's a lot easier. You render the thing and then you use screen for all of those queries. It makes it quite a bit easier. You no longer have to update your destructuring as you need to access new queries or anything like that. That's one major impact, but very subtle change.

[1:45] Another thing is we recommend using userEvent rather than fireEvent most of the time. I do introduce you to userEvent, but I used fireEvent in the videos, because I wasn't totally solid on whether userEvent was going to deliver on the promises that it made. It totally has. It's a really awesome package. I've updated everything in this repo to use userEvent rather than fireEvent.

[2:10] For the most part, that's really, really basic small thing. For things like updating the name or using fireEvent.change, that's now going to be userEvent.type, which is, I think, a lot more sensible. It makes a lot more sense. Then fireEvent.click is now userEvent.click. That's another change.

[2:29] Then finally, in the latest versions of React Testing Library, we've switched wait for waitFor. That was literally a find replace. Everywhere we're calling the wait function, we call waitFor instead. They work basically the same. waitFor has a couple of nice extras built in on top of what we had with wait, so waitFor is the direction forward.

[2:52] One other change that we made here is with the router. I used to recommend that people create a memory history with the history package and then render just a regular router and provide history as a prop to that.

[3:06] I no longer recommend doing it that way. Instead, what we do is we use window.history.pushState, which has nothing to do with the router. This is just like directly updating the URL. We update to the route that we want to be on when we render this page and then we render the regular router.

[3:25] This ends up working out really nicely because you could use the exact same router provider and the exact same routes and everything that you're using in your real app with your tests, which makes things a lot nicer.

[3:37] Now, you just simply say window.history.pushState and here is the route that I want to go to and then you render the regular browser router and outside of that there's nothing else special that you need to do with that, so it's a lot simpler.

[3:50] That's a relatively minor change and it doesn't invalidate the videos at all, but it is a little bit confusing when you look at the master branch and you see that this is a little bit different, so I wanted to call it out specifically.

[4:04] Another thing that we added or changed as part of this is I added another video about MSW. When I recorded the videos, MSW didn't exist. Now, it does and it's awesome, so I've added a brand-new video for that specifically and this is just an improved way to mock out HTTP requests.

[4:23] We do still keep our original Jest mock request here where we're saying jestmock('../api'), but then I show you how you can improve this further using MSW. You can look forward to that. It's a lot better.

[4:36] Hopefully, that was helpful to you, and as you watch through these videos, you can make those changes in your mind. If you see fireEvent, use userEvent instead. Don't accept anything from render, instead just use screen.

[4:50] Like I said, pretty small changes, but significant enough that I wanted to get this information to you, so you don't have to wait for me to get all the videos updated because that takes some time.

[5:02] I hope that you totally love this. Have a good time learning all about testing, reacts, components, and applications. We'll see you around the Internet.

fields.title: Configure Prettier
fields.summary:
1. Prettier is a pretty opinionated tool, but it does allow for some customization. In this lesson we’ll check out [the prettier playground](https://prettier.io/playground/) and see what options we want to enable in our project’s `.prettierrc` file. 
2. After adding in our custom configuration, we’ll create a `.prettierignore` file so that you can avoid formatting any files generated within the project such as `node_modules` or a `build` folder.
fields.transcript:
Instructor: [00:00] The Prettier project has a playground that you can play around with up in here. Actually, there's a bunch of configuration that you can specify Prettier to format things the way that you want it to be formatted.

[00:12] What I'm going to do is I'll go to our example file here and copy all of this. We'll paste it in here. We'll configure Prettier until the output looks the way that I want it to be. We'll go ahead and skip the passer here because Prettier will automatically determine what passer to use based on the file extension.

[00:27] The print width, I like 80. Tab width, that's two. That's good. I don't like tabs. We'll do single quote here. That's going to change the quotes from double to single. I don't like bracket spacing. I'll turn that on. Prose wrap, so this is for markdown. We're going to go ahead and say always wrap.

[00:47] For JavaScript, I don't like semi-colons. You do you. My preference is no semi-colons. JSX single quote, I like double quotes with JSX. I don't like this bracket same line. You can play around with that if you want to. I like the as needed here. Arrow parens, let's avoid those. trailing comma. Let's do all that makes our git disc look a little bit nicer.

[01:13] The HTML white space sensitivity, yeah, that looks fine. I don't want to insert the Prettier format pragma or require that that pragma exists. That's just a comment that goes above the code. I don't want to have that. We'll leave those off.

[01:25] Great. Now it's looking exactly like I want it. I'm going to copy the config JSON. Then I'm going to go in here and the root of my project we'll do. prettierrc, and we'll paste that configuration right in here. Perfect.

[01:40] Now if I run MPM run format, Prettier will automatically pick up this configuration, and it automatically reformats this file to be exactly the way that I like it.

[01:50] In review, all that we needed to do here was we went to the prettier.io/playground app. We configured Prettier on the left side here to do to the code whatever it is that we want to. Then we copied that configuration, pasted it in a Prettier RC file, and ran our format script again, to update our file to be formatted the way that we want it to.

fields.title: Run ESLint with npm Scripts
fields.summary: Let’s add an npm script called lint so we can run ESLint whenever we want with the proper command line flags.
fields.transcript:
Instructor: [00:00] I'm really happy with ESLint, but I really want to make sure that people don't forget to run ESLint. One thing that I can do is add the ESLint script to my scripts configuration here in my package.json. I'll add a lint script. We'll run ESLint on my code base. Now I can run npm run lint. It lints my code base, which is great.

[00:20] We actually have one problem here. That is if I run npm run build, then that's going to build the project, put it out in the dist directory. This generates my output for what I'm going to send to the end user. Now if I run npm run lint, I'm going to get linting errors because Babel is going to add use strict to my output.

[00:45] I really don't actually care to lint the dist directory, the built version of my library. What I'm going to do is I'm going to add a ESLint ignore file right here. With that, I'm actually going to just copy this gitignore. We'll just ignore everything that we have in our gitignore.

[01:02] Now I can run npm run lint. It's working great, but it seems wasteful to have my ESLint ignore file be exactly what my gitignore file is. That's actually often the case. What I'm going to do instead is we'll get rid of this file. We'll go to our package.json. I'll add a flag ignore path .gitignore.

[01:24] I'm basically saying, "Hey, ESLint. When you're looking for the ignore file for the list of directories and files that you should ignore, I want you to use the gitignore file." That way, as I make updates to the gitignore file, I'm not linting anything that is ignored in Git anyway, which is probably going to be pretty consistent for the lifetime of the project.

[01:43] Now I can run npm run lint. That runs ESLint with that ignore path flag. I get exactly the output that I'm expecting. In review, to make all of this work, we added a lint script to our scripts configuration in our package.json. In here, we run ESLint with ignore path pointing to the gitignore file.

[02:03] Then we run it across our project. It's linting across all files that are JavaScript files that are not in the dist or node modules directory. That way, we can be pretty confident that we don't have any code that is violating some ESLint rules that we've configured.

fields.title: Use pre-built ESLint Configuration using extends
fields.summary: ESLint has [a TON of rules](https://eslint.org/docs/rules/) you can configure for your project. But who has time to do all that? This is why ESLint has an extends configuration which you can use to base your own configuration off of other configurations. Let’s add all of ESLint’s built-in recommended rules to our project and override just the ones we want to change.
fields.transcript:
Instructor: [00:00] As cool as it is that ESLint is so customizable that I can configure every single one of the rules, ESLint has a lot of rules, like a ton of built-in rules. There're even rules that you can install from third-party packages.

[00:14] I don't want to have to configure every single one of these rules. It'd be really nice if somebody just gave me a configuration that was like a recommended configuration.

[00:23] ESLint does have that. Instead of configuring each one of these rules, we can say extends, and specify a rule set that we want to extend. ESLint ships with a rule set, and it's called eslint:recommended.

[00:38] With that, we don't need any of these rules here, except for the strict rule here. We want to override the recommended configuration because, in our case, we never want to have [inaudible] . We'll leave that one in there. Our rule configuration that we specify in here will override anything that's in the extends array here.

[00:58] If we wanted to extend multiple configurations, we just add more to the array here. The one that comes last will be the one that overrides the previous. That's how the configuration is merged across these different configurations.

[01:11] With that, I can go ahead and save. Look at my example, and I'm still getting all of the same warnings that I was getting before except I didn't have to configure each one of these rules manually myself.

[01:23] Let's go ahead and we'll fix all these. I'll run npx ESLint.fix. You get all those autofixes in there. Then we'll manually fix a couple of these other ones. That's going to be string. Let's get rid of those extra lines.

[01:36] This one needs to be a template literal that takes care of that one. This one needs just a semicolon in front of it, so we make sure we avoid that unexpected multi-line. With that, now our code is looking much better and our configuration is looking better as well.

[01:50] In review, all that we did here was we used ESLint's built-in recommended configuration. Then we could override some of those rules ourselves in our own custom configuration.
